title: Largest Element
brute: Iterate through array, keep track of max element seen so far. Return max.
better: Same as brute — O(n) is already optimal for unsorted array.
optimal: Single pass: max = arr[0], iterate and update if arr[i] > max. Return max.
trick: Always initialize max with first element, not 0 — handles negative numbers.

title: Second Largest Element
brute: Sort the array descending, return element at index 1. Handle duplicates.
better: Two passes: first find max, second find largest element smaller than max.
optimal: Single pass: track largest and secondLargest. Update both as you iterate.
trick: Initialize both as -infinity. Update secondLargest when you find new max.

title: Check if the Array is Sorted II
brute: Check all adjacent pairs. If any arr[i] > arr[i+1] found more than once, return false.
better: Count the number of places where arr[i] > arr[i+1]. If count > 1, not sorted+rotated.
optimal: Count violations. A sorted+rotated array has at most 1 violation. Also check wraparound arr[n-1] > arr[0].
trick: A sorted rotated array has exactly 0 or 1 'drop' points.
similar: Find minimum in Rotated Sorted Array

title: Remove duplicates from Sorted array
brute: Use a Set to store unique elements, copy back to array.
better: Two pointers on sorted array — no extra space needed.
optimal: i=0 (slow), j=1 (fast). If arr[i] != arr[j], increment i and set arr[i]=arr[j]. Return i+1.
trick: Only works because array is sorted — duplicates are adjacent.
similar: Remove duplicates from sorted array II

title: Left Rotate Array by One
brute: Store first element, shift all elements left by 1, put stored element at end.
optimal: temp = arr[0]. Shift arr[i] = arr[i+1] for i in 0..n-2. arr[n-1] = temp.
trick: Left rotate by 1 = reverse whole array approach is overkill here. Simple shift works.
similar: Left Rotate Array by K Places

title: Left Rotate Array by K Places
brute: Rotate by 1, K times. O(n*k) time.
better: Store first K elements in temp array, shift rest left, put temp at end.
optimal: Reverse first K elements, reverse remaining n-K elements, reverse entire array. K = K % n.
trick: The 3-reversal trick: reverse(0,k-1), reverse(k,n-1), reverse(0,n-1). Remember K%n for large K.

title: Move Zeros to End
brute: Collect non-zero elements in temp array, copy back, fill rest with zeros.
optimal: j points to first zero. i scans forward. When arr[i] != 0, swap arr[i] and arr[j], increment j.
trick: Same idea as Dutch National Flag. j is the 'write pointer' for non-zero values.

title: Linear Search
brute: Iterate through array. Return index when element found. Return -1 if not found.
better: Same — O(n) is only option for unsorted array.
optimal: for i in range(n): if arr[i] == target: return i. return -1.
trick: Use binary search only if array is sorted. Linear search is the baseline.

title: Union of two sorted arrays
brute: Put all elements in a Set, convert to sorted array.
better: Two pointers on both sorted arrays, merge like merge sort step.
optimal: i=0, j=0. Compare arr1[i] and arr2[j]. Add smaller to result (skip duplicates). Advance pointer.
trick: Skip duplicates by checking if last added element equals current before adding.

title: Find missing number
brute: For each number 1 to n, check if it exists in array. O(n²).
better: Sort array, check if arr[i] == i+1 at each position.
optimal: Expected sum = n*(n+1)/2. Actual sum = sum of array. Missing = expected - actual. OR use XOR.
trick: XOR approach: XOR all indices AND all values. Result is the missing number. Handles overflow.
similar: Find the repeating and missing number

title: Maximum Consecutive Ones
brute: For each position, count consecutive ones starting there. Track max.
optimal: count=0, max=0. If arr[i]==1: count++, max=max(max,count). Else count=0.
trick: Reset counter to 0 when you hit a 0. Very simple — don't overthink it.
similar: Longest subarray with given sum K

title: Two Sum
brute: Check all pairs i,j where i<j. If arr[i]+arr[j]==target return [i,j]. O(n²).
better: Sort + two pointers. But loses original indices.
optimal: HashMap: for each element, check if (target - arr[i]) exists in map. If yes return indices. Else add arr[i] to map.
trick: Store VALUE:INDEX in map. Check complement before inserting to handle same element used twice.

title: Sort an array of 0's 1's and 2's
brute: Count 0s, 1s, 2s. Fill array with counts.
better: Same as brute but two passes.
optimal: Dutch National Flag: low=0, mid=0, high=n-1. If arr[mid]=0: swap(low,mid), low++,mid++. If 1: mid++. If 2: swap(mid,high), high--.
trick: Three pointers: low, mid, high. Only move mid when arr[mid]=1 or after swap with low.

title: Majority Element-I
brute: Count frequency of each element using HashMap. Return element with count > n/2.
better: Sort array. Middle element is always majority.
optimal: Boyer-Moore Voting: count=0, candidate=None. If count=0: candidate=arr[i]. If arr[i]==candidate: count++. Else count--.
trick: BM Voting works because majority element appears more than n/2 times — it can't be 'outvoted'. Verify candidate in second pass if needed.
similar: Find the element appearing more than n/3 times

title: Kadane's Algorithm
brute: Check all subarrays, compute sum, track max. O(n²) or O(n³).
optimal: maxSum=arr[0], currentSum=arr[0]. For each element: currentSum=max(arr[i], currentSum+arr[i]). maxSum=max(maxSum, currentSum).
trick: Key insight: if currentSum becomes negative, discard it and start fresh from current element. Negative prefix never helps.

title: Stock Buy and Sell
brute: Try all pairs of buy/sell days. O(n²).
better: Track minimum price seen so far.
optimal: minPrice=prices[0], maxProfit=0. For each price: maxProfit=max(maxProfit, price-minPrice). minPrice=min(minPrice, price).
trick: You don't need to find the exact buy/sell days. Just track min so far and compute profit at each step.

title: Next Permutation
brute: Generate all permutations, sort, find next one.
optimal: 1) Find rightmost index i where arr[i] < arr[i+1]. 2) Find rightmost j where arr[j] > arr[i]. 3) Swap arr[i] and arr[j]. 4) Reverse from i+1 to end.
trick: Step 1 finds the 'dip'. Step 4 reversal is because elements after i are in descending order — reversing makes them ascending (smallest next permutation).

title: Longest Consecutive Sequence in an Array
brute: For each element, check if consecutive sequence exists. O(n²) or O(n³).
better: Sort array. Check consecutive elements. O(n log n).
optimal: Put all elements in HashSet. For each num, if num-1 NOT in set (start of sequence), count sequence length by checking num+1, num+2...
trick: Only start counting from sequence START (num-1 not in set). Avoids redundant work.

title: Set Matrix Zeroes
brute: Find all zeros, mark rows/cols with -1, then set -1 to 0. Risk of false marking.
better: Use extra row[] and col[] boolean arrays to track which rows/cols to zero.
optimal: Use first row and first column as markers. Handle (0,0) separately with a flag variable.
trick: Use the matrix itself as marker storage. First row/col act as boolean flags — saves O(m+n) space.

title: Rotate matrix by 90 degrees
brute: Create new matrix, place elements in rotated positions.
optimal: 1) Transpose matrix (swap arr[i][j] with arr[j][i]). 2) Reverse each row.
trick: Clockwise 90° = Transpose then reverse rows. Counter-clockwise = Transpose then reverse columns.

title: Print the matrix in spiral manner
brute: Mark visited cells, simulate direction change.
optimal: Use 4 boundaries: top, bottom, left, right. Traverse right→down→left→up, shrink boundaries after each direction.
trick: Shrink boundaries after each side traversal. Check top<=bottom and left<=right before each traversal direction.

title: Count subarrays with given sum
brute: Check all subarrays, compute sum, count those equal to k. O(n²).
better: Prefix sum array + nested loop. O(n²).
optimal: Prefix sum + HashMap. For each position, check if (prefixSum - k) exists in map. Count occurrences.
trick: Initialize map with {0:1} to handle subarrays starting from index 0. Key insight: subarray sum = prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i] = k means prefixSum[i] = prefixSum[j] - k.
similar: Count subarrays with given XOR K

title: Pascal's Triangle I
brute: Use nCr formula for each element. Compute factorials.
better: Build row by row. Each element = sum of two above.
optimal: For each row r, start with 1. Each element = prev * (r-col) / col. Build iteratively.
trick: Element at row r, col c = C(r-1, c-1). You can compute each row from previous using: element[j] = element[j-1] * (r-j) / j.

title: Majority Element-II
brute: Count frequency of all elements using HashMap. Return those with count > n/3.
better: Sort. Elements appearing > n/3 times must be at n/3 and 2n/3 positions.
optimal: Extended Boyer-Moore with TWO candidates and TWO counters. Then verify both candidates.
trick: At most 2 elements can appear > n/3 times. Use 2 candidate slots. MUST verify candidates in second pass.
similar: Find element appearing more than n/4 times

title: 3 Sum
brute: Three nested loops. Check all triplets. O(n³).
better: Fix one element, use HashMap for two sum on rest. O(n²) with extra space.
optimal: Sort array. Fix i, use two pointers j=i+1, k=n-1. Move pointers based on sum vs target. Skip duplicates.
trick: Sort first — enables two pointer. Skip duplicates: after fixing i skip same values. After finding triplet, skip same values for j and k too.

title: 4 Sum
brute: Four nested loops. O(n⁴).
better: Fix two elements, two sum for rest. O(n³) with HashSet.
optimal: Sort. Fix i and j (two outer loops). Two pointers for k and l. Skip duplicates at all levels.
trick: Extension of 3Sum with one more outer loop. Same duplicate-skipping logic. Use long to avoid integer overflow.

title: Merge Overlapping Subintervals
brute: Compare each interval with every other. Merge overlapping ones. Repeat until stable.
optimal: Sort by start. Initialize result with first interval. For each next interval: if start <= result.last.end, merge (update end to max). Else add as new interval.
trick: After sorting, only need to compare with LAST interval in result. Two intervals overlap if start2 <= end1.

title: Count Inversions
brute: Check all pairs (i,j) where i<j and arr[i]>arr[j]. Count them. O(n²).
optimal: Modified Merge Sort. During merge step, when right element is placed before left elements, all remaining left elements form inversions. Count them.
pattern: Divide and Conquer — modified merge sort
trick: During merge: when arr[right] < arr[left], inversions += (mid - left + 1). This counts all left elements paired with current right element.

title: Reverse Pairs
brute: Check all pairs (i,j) where i<j and arr[i] > 2*arr[j]. O(n²).
optimal: Modified Merge Sort. Before merging, count pairs using two pointers across left and right halves. Then merge normally.
pattern: Divide and Conquer — modified merge sort
trick: Count BEFORE merging (unlike Count Inversions which counts DURING merge). Use separate counting step with two pointers.

title: Maximum Product Subarray in an Array
brute: Check all subarrays, compute product, track max. O(n²).
optimal: Track both maxProduct and minProduct (negative*negative=positive). At each step: newMax=max(arr[i], maxProd*arr[i], minProd*arr[i]). Update both max and min.
pattern: Dynamic Programming — track min and max
trick: Must track minimum because negative*negative=positive. Reset both to arr[i] if arr[i] alone is better. Handle zeros — they reset the product.

title: Search X in sorted array
brute: Linear search. O(n).
optimal: low=0, high=n-1. While low<=high: mid=(low+high)/2. If arr[mid]==target return mid. If arr[mid]<target: low=mid+1. Else high=mid-1.
trick: Use mid = low + (high-low)/2 to avoid integer overflow. Condition is low<=high (not low<high).

title: Lower Bound
brute: Linear scan from left, return first index where arr[i] >= target.
optimal: Binary search variant. ans=n. While low<=high: if arr[mid]>=target: ans=mid, high=mid-1. Else low=mid+1.
trick: Lower bound = first position where arr[i] >= target. Always save mid as potential answer and search left half.

title: Upper Bound
brute: Linear scan, return first index where arr[i] > target.
optimal: ans=n. While low<=high: if arr[mid]>target: ans=mid, high=mid-1. Else low=mid+1.
trick: Upper bound = first position where arr[i] > target (strictly greater, not >=). One char difference from lower bound.

title: Search insert position
brute: Linear scan to find position.
optimal: Binary search. If found return index. If not found, return low (low will be at insertion point when loop ends).
trick: Same as Lower Bound. When element not found, low is exactly where it should be inserted.

title: Search in rotated sorted array-I
brute: Linear search. O(n).
better: Find pivot, then binary search in appropriate half.
optimal: Binary search. Check which half is sorted: if arr[low]<=arr[mid] (left sorted): check if target in [low,mid], adjust accordingly. Else right is sorted: check if target in [mid,high].
trick: One half is ALWAYS sorted. Identify which half, check if target lies there, eliminate the other half.
similar: Search in rotated sorted array II
Find minimum in Rotated Sorted Array

title: Find minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
brute: Linear scan for minimum. O(n).
optimal: Binary search. If arr[low]<=arr[high] — this half is sorted, min=arr[low]. Else if left sorted (arr[low]<=arr[mid]): min candidate is arr[low], search right. Else search left.
trick: Minimum is at the rotation point. Left sorted = min is arr[low], but rotation is in right half. Update ans and move to unsorted half.
similar: Find how many times array is rotated

title: Koko eating bananas
brute: Try every speed from 1 to max(piles). Check if feasible. O(max*n).
optimal: Binary search on speed (1 to max). For each mid speed, calculate hours needed = sum(ceil(pile/mid)). If hours<=h: feasible, try lower speed. Else try higher.
trick: When you see 'minimum speed/capacity to do X in Y time', think Binary Search on Answer. The feasibility check is monotonic.

title: Aggressive Cows
brute: Try all possible minimum distances. Check if k cows can be placed.
optimal: Sort stalls. Binary search on distance (1 to max_stall-min_stall). For each mid distance, greedily check if k cows can be placed with min gap=mid.
pattern: Binary Search on Answer + Greedy check
trick: Maximize the minimum distance — classic BS on answer pattern. Greedy check: place cow at first stall, then next stall with gap >= mid distance.

title: Median of 2 sorted arrays
brute: Merge both arrays, find median. O(n+m).
better: Use two pointers to find (n+m)/2 th element without full merge. O(n+m).
optimal: Binary search on smaller array. Partition both arrays such that left halves combined have (n+m)/2 elements. Check max of lefts <= min of rights.
trick: Always binary search on SMALLER array. Partition condition: max(leftA, leftB) <= min(rightA, rightB). If violated, adjust partition.
similar: Kth element of two sorted arrays

title: Search in a 2D matrix
brute: Linear search entire matrix. O(m*n).
better: Binary search each row. O(m log n).
optimal: Treat matrix as flattened sorted array. Binary search with mid = (low+high)/2. Convert: row=mid/n, col=mid%n.
trick: Only works if matrix rows are sorted AND first element of each row > last of previous. Use index mapping: (row, col) = (mid/cols, mid%cols).

title: Reverse a LinkedList
brute: Store values in array, create new list in reverse order.
optimal: Iterative: prev=null, curr=head. While curr: next=curr.next, curr.next=prev, prev=curr, curr=next. Return prev.
trick: Three variables: prev, curr, next. Visualize arrows reversing one by one. After loop, prev is new head.
similar: Reverse LL in groups of K

title: Detect a loop in LL
brute: Use HashSet to store visited nodes. If node seen again, cycle exists.
optimal: Floyd's cycle detection: slow and fast pointers. slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. If they meet, cycle exists. If fast reaches null, no cycle.
trick: Tortoise and Hare. They MUST meet inside the cycle if cycle exists — fast pointer gains 1 step per iteration relative to slow.

title: Find the starting point of loop in LL
brute: HashSet — first repeated node is the start.
optimal: Floyd's: find meeting point. Then move one pointer to head. Move both one step at a time. They meet at loop start.
trick: Mathematical proof: distance from head to loop start = distance from meeting point to loop start. So reset one pointer to head and walk both at speed 1.

title: Combination Sum
brute: Generate all subsets with repetition, filter those summing to target.
optimal: Backtracking: pick element (can repeat), recurse with remaining sum. If sum=0: add to result. If sum<0 or index out of bounds: return.
trick: Since repetition allowed, don't increment index when picking. Sort candidates first to enable early pruning (break when candidate > remaining sum).

title: N Queen
brute: Place queens in all possible positions, check validity.
optimal: Backtracking row by row. For each column in current row, check if safe (no conflict in same col, upper-left diagonal, upper-right diagonal). Place and recurse.
trick: Use three HashSets/arrays to track: occupied columns, left diagonals (row-col), right diagonals (row+col). O(1) safety check instead of O(n).

title: Balanced Parenthesis
brute: Count opens and closes. Doesn't handle ordering.
optimal: Stack: for open bracket push. For close bracket: if stack empty or top doesn't match, return false. Pop if matches. Return stack.isEmpty().
trick: Push open brackets. On close bracket, check if stack top is matching open. Empty stack at end = valid.

title: Next Greater Element
brute: For each element, scan right to find next greater. O(n²).
optimal: Monotonic decreasing stack. Traverse right to left (or left to right with stack). Maintain stack of elements waiting for their next greater. When larger element found, pop and assign.
trick: Traverse RIGHT TO LEFT. Push to stack. Current element's NGE = stack top (if stack not empty). Pop elements smaller than current (they won't be NGE for anything to the left).

title: Trapping Rainwater
brute: For each index, find maxLeft and maxRight by scanning. Water at i = min(maxL, maxR) - height[i]. O(n²).
better: Precompute leftMax[] and rightMax[] arrays. O(n) time, O(n) space.
optimal: Two pointers: left=0, right=n-1. Track leftMax, rightMax. If height[left]<height[right]: process left side. Else process right side.
trick: Water at position i = min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i]. Two pointer: always process the SHORTER side — it determines the water level.

title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
brute: For each pair of bars, compute rectangle. O(n²).
better: For each bar, find left and right boundary using next smaller element.
optimal: Monotonic increasing stack. Push indices. When height decreases: pop and compute area = height[popped] * (right - left - 1). Process remaining stack at end.
trick: Stack stores bars in increasing order. When we find smaller bar, it's the RIGHT boundary. Left boundary = new stack top. Width = right - left - 1.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
brute: Generate all substrings, check each for uniqueness. O(n³).
better: Sliding window with HashSet. Shrink from left when duplicate found.
optimal: Sliding window with HashMap storing last index. When duplicate found, move left to max(left, lastSeen[char]+1). Track maxLen.
trick: HashMap stores last seen index. Jump left pointer directly to (lastSeen+1) instead of shrinking one by one. More efficient than HashSet approach.

title: Climbing stairs
brute: Recursion: f(n) = f(n-1) + f(n-2). Exponential time.
optimal: Bottom-up DP or just two variables (Fibonacci): a=1, b=1. For i in 2..n: c=a+b, a=b, b=c. Return b.
trick: Exactly Fibonacci sequence. dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Optimize to O(1) space with two variables.
similar: Count ways to reach nth stair

title: House Robber
brute: Try all subsets of non-adjacent houses. Pick max sum.
optimal: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]). Optimize: prev2=0, prev1=0. For each num: curr=max(prev1, prev2+num). Update prev2=prev1, prev1=curr.
trick: At each house: either skip it (take prev1) or rob it (take prev2+current). Classic DP recurrence. Optimize to two variables.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
brute: Generate all subsequences of both strings, find longest common one.
optimal: 2D DP: dp[i][j] = LCS of s1[0..i] and s2[0..j]. If s1[i]==s2[j]: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]+1. Else: dp[i][j]=max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
trick: Base case: empty string LCS = 0. Fill table row by row. Space optimize by using only two rows at a time.

title: Edit Distance
brute: Try all possible edit sequences recursively.
optimal: dp[i][j] = min edits to convert s1[0..i] to s2[0..j]. If chars match: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]. Else: dp[i][j]=1+min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]).
trick: Three operations: insert (dp[i][j-1]), delete (dp[i-1][j]), replace (dp[i-1][j-1]). When chars match: free operation = dp[i-1][j-1].

title: Number of provinces
brute: DFS/BFS from each unvisited node. Count how many times you start a new traversal.
optimal: DFS/BFS or Union-Find. Count connected components. For each unvisited node: DFS to mark all connected as visited, increment count.
trick: Number of provinces = number of connected components. Simple DFS/BFS counting. OR use Union-Find for cleaner code.

title: Number of Islands
brute: N/A
optimal: For each unvisited '1': DFS/BFS to mark all connected '1's as visited, increment island count. Use 4-directional movement.
trick: Modify grid in-place: mark visited '1's as '0' to avoid extra visited array. 4 directions: up, down, left, right.

title: Course Schedule I
brute: Try all orderings, check if valid.
optimal: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS Topo Sort): compute in-degrees. Add 0 in-degree nodes to queue. Process queue: reduce in-degree of neighbors. If all nodes processed = no cycle = can finish.
pattern: Topological Sort / Cycle Detection in Directed Graph
trick: If topological sort processes all nodes = no cycle = return true. If some nodes unprocessed = cycle exists = return false. Kahn's is iterative, easier than DFS for this.

title: Climbing stairs
brute: Recursion: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2).
optimal: Two variables: a=1, b=2. For i in 3..n: c=a+b, a=b, b=c. Return b.
trick: Identical to Fibonacci. f(1)=1, f(2)=2, f(n)=f(n-1)+f(n-2).

title: Rotten Oranges
brute: Simulate minute by minute. O(m*n * minutes).
optimal: Multi-source BFS: add all initially rotten oranges to queue. BFS level by level (each level = 1 minute). Track fresh count. Answer = levels processed if fresh=0 else -1.
trick: Add ALL rotten oranges to queue initially — they all rot simultaneously. BFS naturally handles level-by-level (minute-by-minute) processing.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
brute: Generate all subsequences, check if increasing, find longest. O(2^n).
better: DP: dp[i] = LIS ending at index i. For each i, check all j<i where arr[j]<arr[i]. O(n²).
optimal: Binary search approach using patience sorting. Maintain a tails array. For each element: binary search for first tail >= element, replace it. Length of tails = LIS length.
trick: Tails array doesn't give actual LIS but its LENGTH. Replace first element >= current (using lower_bound). Each replacement maintains smallest possible tail for each length.

title: Bubble Sort
brute: N/A — this IS the brute force sort.
better: Add a flag to detect if any swap happened. If no swap in a pass, array is sorted — exit early.
optimal: Outer loop n times. Inner loop compares adjacent pairs, swaps if out of order. Each pass bubbles largest element to end.
time_complexity: O(n²) worst, O(n) best (sorted array with flag)
trick: After each outer pass, last i elements are sorted — inner loop can reduce range. Add swapped flag for early exit.

title: Selection Sort
brute: N/A
optimal: For each position i: find minimum in arr[i..n-1], swap with arr[i]. Repeat for all positions.
trick: Always O(n²) — no early exit possible. Minimum number of swaps among simple sorts (at most n-1 swaps). Not stable.

title: Insertion Sort
brute: N/A
optimal: For each element from index 1: compare with elements before it, shift larger ones right, insert element in correct position.
trick: Best for nearly sorted arrays. Stable sort. Used internally by Timsort for small arrays. Think: sorting playing cards in hand.

title: Merge Sort
brute: N/A
optimal: Divide array in half recursively until single elements. Merge sorted halves by comparing elements one by one. Build up sorted array.
trick: Stable sort. Preferred for linked lists (no random access needed). Used in Count Inversions and Reverse Pairs. Always O(n log n) — no worst case.

title: Quick Sort
brute: N/A
optimal: Pick pivot. Partition: elements < pivot go left, > pivot go right. Recursively sort left and right partitions.
time_complexity: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst
trick: Worst case avoided with random pivot or median-of-three. Not stable. Fastest in practice due to cache performance. Partition step is key — Lomuto vs Hoare.

title: Find square root of a number
brute: Try all numbers from 1 to n. Return largest whose square <= n.
optimal: Binary search on answer (1 to n). If mid*mid <= n: ans=mid, low=mid+1. Else high=mid-1. For decimal precision: iterate fixed times adjusting by 0.1, 0.01 etc.
trick: Use long for mid*mid to avoid overflow. For decimal answer, do three nested loops with step 1, 0.1, 0.01 etc.
similar: Find Nth root of a number

title: Book Allocation Problem
brute: Try all possible maximum page limits. Check feasibility.
optimal: Binary search on answer (max pages: max(arr) to sum(arr)). For each mid: greedily allocate books checking if k students can read. Minimize the maximum.
pattern: Binary Search on Answer + Greedy feasibility
trick: Lower bound = max single book (must give at least one book to someone). Upper = sum (one student reads all). Greedy check: assign books until student's limit exceeded, move to next student.

title: Split array - largest sum
brute: Try all ways to split array into k parts.
optimal: Binary search on answer. Same as Book Allocation — minimize the maximum subarray sum when split into k parts.
trick: Identical to Book Allocation. Minimize the maximum = binary search pattern. Feasibility check: greedy partitioning.

title: Single element in a Sorted Array
brute: XOR all elements. Result is the single element.
optimal: Binary search. Pairs are at (even, odd) indices before single element and (odd, even) after. Check: if arr[mid]==arr[mid+1]: single is in right half (low=mid+2). Else: single is in left half or at mid (high=mid).
trick: Before single element: pairs at (even, odd). After: pairs at (odd, even). Ensure mid is even before comparing with mid+1.
similar: Find minimum in rotated sorted array

title: Find peak element
brute: Linear scan. Return index where arr[i] > arr[i-1] and arr[i] > arr[i+1].
optimal: Binary search. If arr[mid] > arr[mid+1]: peak is in left half (include mid). Else: peak is in right half. Return low when low==high.
trick: Any local maximum is a valid answer. If right neighbor is larger, there must be a peak to the right (array eventually ends or turns). Move toward the larger side.

title: Rearrange array elements by sign
brute: Separate positives and negatives. Interleave them.
optimal: Two pointers on result array: posIdx=0, negIdx=1. Iterate original: if positive place at posIdx, posIdx+=2. If negative place at negIdx, negIdx+=2.
trick: Positives go to even indices (0,2,4...), negatives to odd indices (1,3,5...). Fill simultaneously in one pass.

title: Leaders in an Array
brute: For each element, check if all elements to its right are smaller. O(n²).
optimal: Traverse right to left. Track running maximum from right. Element is leader if it's >= current max from right. Update max.
trick: Rightmost element is always a leader. Traverse backwards — if current > maxFromRight, it's a leader. Reverse result at end.

title: Largest Subarray with Sum 0
brute: Check all subarrays, compute sum, find longest with sum 0. O(n²).
optimal: Prefix sum + HashMap. If same prefix sum appears at indices i and j, subarray (i+1, j) has sum 0. Store first occurrence of each prefix sum. Track max length.
trick: Initialize map with {0: -1}. If prefixSum already in map, length = i - map[prefixSum]. Don't update map if key exists (want FIRST occurrence for maximum length).

title: Count subarrays with given xor K
brute: Check all subarrays, compute XOR, count those equal to K. O(n²).
optimal: Prefix XOR + HashMap. For each position, check if (prefixXOR ^ K) exists in map. Count = map[prefixXOR ^ K]. Similar to subarray sum = K.
trick: XOR property: if prefixXOR[j] ^ prefixXOR[i] = K, then subarray (i+1,j) has XOR = K. Same structure as subarray sum problem but with XOR.

title: Merge two sorted arrays without extra space
brute: Merge into third array, copy back. O(n+m) space.
better: Two pointers from end of arr1 and arr2. Place larger at end of combined.
optimal: Gap method (Shell sort based): gap = ceil((n+m)/2). Compare elements gap apart, swap if out of order. Reduce gap by half each iteration until gap=1.
trick: Gap method: start with large gap, elements far apart get sorted first. Gap = ceil(gap/2) each iteration. Takes O(log(n+m)) iterations.

title: Find the repeating and missing number
brute: Count frequency of each number 1 to n. Repeating has count 2, missing has count 0. O(n).
better: Math: sum of array vs expected sum gives (repeating-missing). Sum of squares gives another equation. Solve both.
optimal: XOR approach: XOR all elements and 1..n. Result = repeating XOR missing. Use rightmost set bit to separate into two groups. Find both numbers.
trick: Math approach: let repeating=A, missing=B. S-Sn = A-B. S²-Sn² = A²-B². Divide to get A+B. Solve two equations. Simpler than XOR for interviews.

title: Check if LL is palindrome
brute: Store values in array. Check if array is palindrome.
optimal: 1) Find middle with slow/fast pointers. 2) Reverse second half. 3) Compare both halves. 4) Restore list (optional).
trick: Combination of two LL techniques: find middle + reverse. After comparison, restore by reversing second half again (good practice).

title: Remove Nth node from back of LL
brute: Find length. Remove (length-N+1)th node from front.
optimal: Two pointers. Move fast N steps ahead. Then move both until fast reaches end. Slow is now at node before target. Skip the target node.
trick: Use dummy node before head to handle edge case of removing head. Gap of N between fast and slow ensures slow stops at correct position.

title: Add two numbers in Linked List
brute: Convert to numbers, add, convert back. Fails for very large numbers.
optimal: Traverse both lists simultaneously. Add digits + carry. Create new node for each sum%10. Carry = sum/10. Handle remaining nodes and final carry.
trick: Don't convert to int — lists can represent huge numbers. Handle three cases: both lists have nodes, one exhausted, final carry remaining.

title: Reverse LL in group of given size K
brute: Store in array, reverse in chunks of K, rebuild list.
optimal: Check if K nodes remain. If yes: reverse K nodes using standard reversal. Connect reversed group to recursively processed rest. If no: return head as is.
trick: Check K nodes exist before reversing (don't reverse last incomplete group). Track prev group's tail to connect to next reversed group.

title: Combination Sum II
brute: Generate all subsets, filter unique ones summing to target.
optimal: Sort array. Backtracking: pick each element once. Skip duplicates at same recursion level (if i>start and candidates[i]==candidates[i-1]: skip).
trick: Sort first to group duplicates. Skip condition: i > start (not i > 0) — allows using same value in different recursion branches but not same branch.

title: Subsets II
brute: Generate all subsets, add to Set to remove duplicates.
optimal: Sort array. Backtracking: at each level, skip duplicates (if i>start and nums[i]==nums[i-1]: skip). Add current subset to result at every step.
trick: Same duplicate-skipping trick as Combination Sum II. Sort + skip when i>start and nums[i]==nums[i-1].

title: Sudoku Solver
brute: Try all digit placements for empty cells.
optimal: Backtracking: for each empty cell, try digits 1-9. Check if valid (not in same row, col, 3x3 box). If valid: place digit, recurse. If recursion fails: undo (backtrack).
trick: Box index = (row/3)*3 + col/3. Use three boolean arrays for row, col, box to O(1) validity check. Return true when all cells filled.

title: Rat in a Maze
brute: Try all paths recursively.
optimal: Backtracking: from (0,0) try all 4 directions. Mark cell visited. If reach (n-1,n-1): add path to result. Unmark cell (backtrack).
trick: Mark cell as visited BEFORE recursing to avoid cycles. Unmark AFTER returning (backtrack). Collect path string (D/L/R/U) as you go.

title: Implement Min Stack
brute: Use two stacks: one for values, one for minimums.
optimal: One stack storing modified values. Push: if val >= min, push val. Else push (2*val - min), update min=val. Pop: if top >= min, pop normally. Else min = 2*min - top.
trick: Two-stack approach is simpler and more readable for interviews. Single-stack math trick: encode previous min in the value. 2*val - oldMin restores previous min on pop.

title: Sliding Window Maximum
brute: For each window, find max by scanning all elements. O(n*k).
optimal: Monotonic decreasing deque. Maintain indices of useful elements. Remove from back if smaller than current. Remove from front if outside window. Front always = max of current window.
trick: Store INDICES not values. Remove front when index <= i-k (outside window). Remove back when nums[back] <= nums[i] (useless — current is larger and will outlive them).

title: LRU Cache
brute: Array with timestamp. On eviction, find oldest.
optimal: HashMap + Doubly Linked List. HashMap: key->node for O(1) lookup. DLL: maintains order (most recent at head, LRU at tail). Get: move to head. Put: add to head, evict tail if over capacity.
trick: Use dummy head and tail nodes to avoid null checks. On every access (get/put), move node to front. On capacity overflow, remove tail.prev.

title: Max Consecutive Ones III
brute: Try all subarrays, count zeros, track longest with <= k zeros.
optimal: Sliding window: expand right always. When zeros in window > k: shrink from left. Track max window size.
pattern: Sliding Window — at most K constraint
trick: Count zeros in window. When zeros > k: move left pointer until zeros <= k. Max window = max of all valid windows.

title: Minimum Window Substring
brute: Check all substrings. Find shortest containing all chars of t.
optimal: Sliding window with two HashMaps (need and window). Expand right adding chars. When all required chars satisfied: record window, shrink from left. Track minimum.
pattern: Sliding Window — minimum window with all chars
trick: Track 'formed' count (how many unique chars satisfied). Only shrink when formed == required. Use have/need counters per char.

title: Kth Largest element in an array
brute: Sort descending, return k-1 th element. O(n log n).
better: Min-heap of size k. Iterate: if heap.size < k push. Else if curr > heap.top: pop and push. Top = kth largest.
optimal: Quick Select: partition like quicksort. If pivot at index n-k: found it. Else recurse on correct half. O(n) average.
time_complexity: O(n) average Quick Select, O(n log k) heap
trick: Min-heap of size k: maintains k largest elements seen so far. Top = kth largest. Quick Select is faster on average but O(n²) worst case.

title: Merge K sorted Lists
brute: Collect all values, sort, create new list. O(n log n).
better: Merge lists two at a time. O(kn) total.
optimal: Min-heap of size k. Initialize with head of each list. Extract min: add to result, push next node from that list. Repeat until heap empty.
trick: Always extract the global minimum efficiently using min-heap. Push (value, listIndex, nodePointer) to heap. O(log k) per operation.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
brute: Store all elements, sort when finding median. O(n log n) per query.
better: Keep sorted array, insert in order. O(n) insertion.
optimal: Two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half. Balance sizes. Median = top of max-heap (odd) or average of both tops (even).
trick: Max-heap lower half, min-heap upper half. Always maintain: maxHeap.size == minHeap.size OR maxHeap.size == minHeap.size + 1. Rebalance after each insert.

title: Top K Frequent Elements
brute: Count frequencies, sort by frequency, return top k.
better: Min-heap of size k by frequency.
optimal: Count frequencies with HashMap. Use min-heap of size k — maintains top k frequent. OR bucket sort: index=frequency, O(n).
time_complexity: O(n log k) heap, O(n) bucket sort
trick: Bucket sort approach: create array of size n+1 where index = frequency. Collect from highest frequency bucket. O(n) time.

title: Lemonade Change
brute: N/A
optimal: Track count of $5 and $10 bills. For $5: keep. For $10: give one $5, keep $10. For $20: give one $10 + one $5 (prefer) OR three $5s. Return false if can't make change.
trick: For $20 change: prefer $10+$5 over $5+$5+$5 (saves $5 bills which are more versatile). Always make greedily optimal change.

title: Jump Game I
brute: BFS/DFS trying all jump possibilities.
better: DP: dp[i] = can we reach index i. O(n²).
optimal: Greedy: track maxReach. For each index i: if i > maxReach: return false. maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]). Return true if loop completes.
trick: Only need to know if current index is reachable (i <= maxReach) and update max reach. Don't need to track actual path.

title: Jump Game II
brute: BFS counting levels (jumps).
optimal: Greedy: track current jump end and farthest reachable. When i reaches current jump end: make a jump (jumps++), update jump end to farthest. Stop when jump end >= last index.
trick: Think of it as BFS levels. Each 'jump' is a BFS level. Track the farthest reachable in current level. When level exhausted, jump to next level.

title: Insert Interval
brute: Add new interval to list, sort, merge overlapping.
optimal: Three phases: 1) Add all intervals ending before new start. 2) Merge all overlapping with new interval (update new start/end). 3) Add remaining intervals.
trick: No sorting needed since input is already sorted. Overlap condition: existing.end >= new.start AND existing.start <= new.end.

title: Non-overlapping Intervals
brute: Try removing each combination of intervals, check if rest are non-overlapping.
optimal: Sort by end time. Greedily keep interval with earliest end. When overlap: remove interval with later end (keep earlier one — allows more future intervals).
trick: Sort by END time (not start). Keep interval with smallest end — it leaves most room for future intervals. Count removed = overlaps found.

title: Level Order Traversal
brute: DFS with level tracking.
optimal: BFS with queue. Process level by level: get current queue size (= nodes in this level). Process all, add their children. Record each level's values.
trick: Key: capture queue.size() BEFORE inner loop — this is number of nodes at current level. Inner loop processes exactly one level at a time.

title: Maximum Depth in BT
brute: N/A
better: BFS level by level, count levels.
optimal: DFS: if node is null return 0. Return 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)).
trick: Simplest recursive solution. Base case: null = 0. Each node adds 1 to the deeper subtree's depth.

title: Diameter of Binary Tree
brute: For each node compute left height + right height. Track max. O(n²).
optimal: DFS: return height from each node. At each node: diameter through this node = leftHeight + rightHeight. Update global max. Return 1 + max(left, right) as height.
pattern: Tree DFS — compute height while tracking answer
trick: Diameter doesn't have to pass through root. Track global max. Function returns HEIGHT but updates diameter as side effect. Classic pattern: compute one thing, track another.

title: Maximum path sum
brute: Try all paths in tree. O(n²).
optimal: DFS: at each node compute max gain from left and right (ignore negative: max(0, left/right)). Update global max with node + left_gain + right_gain. Return node + max(left, right) gain.
trick: Ignore negative contributions: max(0, dfs(child)). Path through node = node.val + left_gain + right_gain. But return value = node.val + max(left, right) — can only extend path ONE direction up.

title: LCA in BT
brute: Find path from root to p and q. First diverging point is LCA.
optimal: DFS: if node is null/p/q return node. Recurse left and right. If both return non-null: current node is LCA. If one returns null: return the other.
trick: Elegant single-pass solution. If we find p or q, return it immediately — don't search further. LCA is the node where left and right both return non-null.

title: Serialize and De-serialize BT
brute: N/A
optimal: Serialize: BFS/DFS, record values with null markers. Deserialize: split string, reconstruct using same traversal order.
trick: Use preorder (root, left, right) with '#' for nulls. Deserialize: use queue of values, recursively build tree — first value is root, next subtree is left, then right.

title: Insert a given node in BST
brute: N/A
optimal: Recursive: if val < node.val go left, else go right. When null found, create new node. OR iterative with parent pointer.
time_complexity: O(h) where h=height, O(log n) balanced
pattern: BST property — left smaller, right larger
trick: BST insertion always goes to a leaf position. Follow BST property to find correct position. No rebalancing needed for basic BST.

title: Delete a node in BST
brute: N/A
optimal: Find node. Three cases: 1) Leaf: just delete. 2) One child: replace with child. 3) Two children: replace with inorder successor (leftmost of right subtree), delete successor from right subtree.
trick: Inorder successor = smallest node in right subtree = go right once, then keep going left. Replace deleted node's value with successor's value, then delete successor.

title: Check if a tree is a BST or not
brute: For each node check if all left descendants < node and all right > node. O(n²).
optimal: Pass valid range [min, max] to each node. Root: (-inf, +inf). Left child: (-inf, parent.val). Right child: (parent.val, +inf). Fail if node.val outside range.
trick: Inorder traversal of BST gives sorted array — check if inorder is strictly increasing. OR use min/max bounds (cleaner). Common mistake: only checking immediate parent, not entire ancestor range.

title: Bipartite Graph
brute: Try all 2-colorings.
optimal: BFS/DFS graph coloring with 2 colors. Color starting node with color 0. Color all neighbors with color 1. If neighbor already colored same as current: not bipartite.
trick: Bipartite = 2-colorable = no odd-length cycles. BFS coloring: alternate colors. If conflict found (same color adjacent nodes): return false. Handle disconnected components.

title: Word ladder I
brute: Try all possible word sequences.
optimal: BFS from beginWord. At each level: try changing each character to a-z. If valid word in wordList: add to queue (and remove from wordList to avoid revisiting). Count levels to reach endWord.
pattern: BFS — shortest path in word graph
trick: Remove words from set as you visit them (don't use separate visited set — slower). Level count = transformation steps. Early termination when endWord found.

title: Flood fill algorithm
brute: N/A
optimal: DFS/BFS from source pixel. If current pixel == source color: change to new color, recurse on 4 neighbors. Handle edge case: new color == old color (no-op).
trick: Check if newColor == oldColor before starting to avoid infinite recursion. Classic connected component flooding — same as Number of Islands.

title: Course Schedule II
brute: Try all orderings, check validity.
optimal: Kahn's Algorithm: compute in-degrees. BFS from 0 in-degree nodes. Add to result order. Reduce neighbors' in-degrees. If result size == n: return order. Else: cycle exists, return empty.
trick: Topological order = valid course order. If cycle: impossible. DFS alternative: use color states (white/gray/black) — gray = currently visiting = cycle.

title: Cheapest flight within K stops
brute: BFS trying all paths with at most K stops.
optimal: Modified Bellman-Ford: run K+1 iterations (K stops = K+1 edges). Each iteration: relax all edges. Use copy of previous iteration's distances to avoid using more than one new edge per iteration.
trick: Use temporary distance array updated from PREVIOUS iteration's values — prevents using multiple edges in one iteration. K stops = K+1 edges = K+1 Bellman-Ford iterations.
similar: Number of ways to arrive at destination

title: Partition equal subset sum
brute: Try all subsets, check if any sums to total/2.
optimal: 0/1 Knapsack DP. Target = sum/2. dp[i][j] = can we achieve sum j using first i elements. dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] OR dp[i-1][j-nums[i]]. Space optimize to 1D array.
trick: If sum is odd: impossible immediately. Target = sum/2. 1D DP: iterate j from target DOWN to nums[i] to avoid using same element twice.

title: Coin Change
brute: Try all combinations of coins. Exponential.
optimal: DP: dp[i] = minimum coins to make amount i. dp[0]=0. For each amount: for each coin: if coin<=amount: dp[amount]=min(dp[amount], dp[amount-coin]+1).
trick: Initialize dp with infinity except dp[0]=0. Iterate amounts outer, coins inner. Since coins can be reused (unbounded), iterate forwards (unlike 0/1 knapsack).

title: Longest palindromic subsequence
brute: Generate all subsequences, check palindrome, find longest.
optimal: LPS(s) = LCS(s, reverse(s)). OR direct DP: dp[i][j] = LPS of s[i..j]. If s[i]==s[j]: dp[i][j]=dp[i+1][j-1]+2. Else: max(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
trick: LPS = LCS(s, reverse(s)) — elegant reduction. Fill DP table diagonally (by length of substring). Single chars have LPS=1.

title: Best time to buy and sell stock III
brute: Try all pairs of transactions. O(n⁴).
better: For each split point: max profit = best single transaction in left + best single transaction in right.
optimal: DP with states: (day, transactions_used, holding). 4 states: buy1, sell1, buy2, sell2. Track max profit at each state across all days.
trick: State machine: buy1 = max(-price, buy1). sell1 = max(buy1+price, sell1). buy2 = max(sell1-price, buy2). sell2 = max(buy2+price, sell2). Answer = sell2.

title: Longest Divisible Subset
brute: Check all subsets, verify divisibility chain.
optimal: Sort array. LIS-style DP: dp[i] = longest divisible subset ending at i. For j<i: if nums[i]%nums[j]==0: dp[i]=max(dp[i], dp[j]+1). Track parent array to reconstruct.
trick: After sorting: if a divides b and b divides c, then a divides c (transitivity). So longest chain is exactly LIS with divisibility condition. Track prev[] to reconstruct subset.

title: Burst balloons
brute: Try all orders of bursting. O(n!).
optimal: Think in reverse: which balloon is burst LAST in range [i,j]? dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting all in range. For each k as last burst: coins = nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1] + dp[i][k-1] + dp[k+1][j].
trick: Key insight: think about LAST balloon burst, not first. Add 1s at boundaries. Interval DP: fill by increasing length of intervals.

title: Implement Trie
brute: Use HashSet for words. No prefix search.
optimal: Each TrieNode has children[26] and isEnd boolean. Insert: create nodes for each char. Search: traverse chars, return isEnd at last char. StartsWith: traverse chars, return true if all exist.
time_complexity: O(m) for all operations where m=word length
trick: children can be array[26] or HashMap. isEnd flag marks complete words. Prefix search = search but without checking isEnd. Used for autocomplete, spell check.

title: Maximum XOR of two numbers in an array
brute: Check all pairs, compute XOR, track max. O(n²).
optimal: Build binary Trie of all numbers. For each number: traverse Trie greedily choosing opposite bit at each level (to maximize XOR). Track max XOR found.
trick: XOR is maximized when bits are opposite. At each bit level in Trie, try to go to opposite bit node. If not available, go same bit. Build Trie of all numbers first.

title: Reverse a number
brute: Convert to string, reverse, convert back.
optimal: Extract digits using % 10, build reversed number: reversed = reversed*10 + digit. n = n/10 until n=0.
trick: % 10 gives last digit. / 10 removes last digit. Handle overflow check for 32-bit integers. Handle negative numbers separately.

title: Palindrome Number
brute: Convert to string, check if string equals its reverse.
optimal: Reverse only half the number. Compare reversed half with remaining half. Negative numbers are never palindromes. Numbers ending in 0 (except 0 itself) are not palindromes.
trick: Reverse half: while n > reversed: reversed = reversed*10 + n%10, n /= 10. Check n == reversed OR n == reversed/10 (odd digits case).

title: GCD of Two Numbers
brute: Find all factors of both numbers. Return largest common factor.
better: Subtract smaller from larger repeatedly until equal.
optimal: Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a%b). Base case: gcd(a, 0) = a.
trick: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a%b). Very fast — number of steps proportional to log of smaller number. LCM = (a*b) / gcd(a,b).

title: Check for Prime Number
brute: Check divisibility from 2 to n-1. O(n).
better: Check divisibility from 2 to n/2. O(n/2).
optimal: Check divisibility from 2 to sqrt(n). If n has factor > sqrt(n), the paired factor is < sqrt(n) and would already be found.
trick: Factors come in pairs (a, n/a). One of each pair is <= sqrt(n). So only check up to sqrt(n). Handle edge cases: 1 is not prime, 2 is prime.

title: Factorial of a given number
brute: Iterative loop multiplying 1 to n.
optimal: Recursive: f(n) = n * f(n-1). Base: f(0) = f(1) = 1. OR iterative with single variable.
trick: Classic recursion example. Recursion stack depth = n. For large n, use BigInteger or iterative to avoid stack overflow.

title: Fibonacci Number
brute: Recursion: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2). O(2^n).
optimal: Two variables: a=0, b=1. For i in 2..n: c=a+b, a=b, b=c. Return b.
trick: Matrix exponentiation for O(log n). For interviews O(n) with two variables is sufficient and expected.

title: Reverse an array
brute: Create new array, fill in reverse order.
optimal: Recursive: swap(arr[start], arr[end]), recurse with start+1, end-1. OR iterative two pointers: l=0, r=n-1. While l<r: swap, l++, r--.
trick: Classic two pointer. Recursive version: base case l>=r. Same logic, different style. Iterative preferred for space.

title: Remove duplicates from Sorted array
brute: Use HashSet, copy unique elements back.
optimal: Two pointers: i=0 (write), j=1 (read). If arr[j] != arr[i]: i++, arr[i]=arr[j]. j always increments. Return i+1.
trick: Works only on sorted arrays — duplicates are adjacent. Write pointer i only advances when new unique element found.

title: Find the number that appears once and other numbers twice
brute: HashMap frequency count. Return element with count 1.
better: Sort array. Check pairs at even indices.
optimal: XOR all elements. Pairs cancel out (a XOR a = 0). Single element remains (a XOR 0 = a).
trick: XOR is self-inverse. All duplicates cancel. Magic of XOR: a XOR a = 0 and a XOR 0 = a. Works in one pass.

title: Longest subarray with given sum K(positives)
brute: Check all subarrays, compute sum, find longest with sum K. O(n²).
better: Prefix sum + HashMap storing first occurrence.
optimal: Sliding window (works for positives only): expand right, shrink left when sum > K. Track max length when sum == K.
trick: For positives only: sliding window works because adding elements only increases sum. For negatives: use prefix sum + HashMap approach.

title: Print subarray with maximum subarray sum
brute: Check all subarrays, find max sum, track indices.
optimal: Kadane's with index tracking: track start, end, tempStart. When currentSum < 0: reset, update tempStart. When new max found: update start=tempStart, end=i.
trick: Extension of Kadane's. Track tempStart when resetting sum. Update actual start and end only when new maximum found.

title: Zig Zag Traversal
brute: Level order traversal. Reverse alternate levels.
optimal: BFS level by level. Use flag leftToRight. If true: add normally. If false: add to front of deque or reverse. Toggle flag each level.
trick: Use deque for current level. Even levels: addLast. Odd levels: addFirst. Toggle direction flag after each level. No actual reversal needed.

title: Top View of BT
brute: N/A
optimal: BFS with horizontal distance. Root has hd=0. Left child: hd-1. Right child: hd+1. Store first node at each hd in TreeMap. Print in order of hd.
trick: Top view = first node seen at each horizontal distance (BFS ensures top-to-bottom order). Use TreeMap to auto-sort by hd. Only store FIRST node at each hd.

title: Right/Left View of Binary Tree
brute: Level order. Take last element of each level (right view).
optimal: BFS: last node in each level = right view, first node = left view. OR DFS: go right first, track level. Add node when level == result.size() (first node seen at each level).
pattern: BFS level order / DFS with level tracking
trick: DFS trick: traverse right child first. When level == result.size(), it's the first node at this level = rightmost visible. Elegant recursive solution.

title: Construct a BT from Preorder and Inorder
brute: N/A
optimal: Preorder first element = root. Find root in inorder — elements to left = left subtree, right = right subtree. Recursively build both subtrees.
trick: HashMap stores inorder value->index for O(1) root finding. Preorder index tracks current root. Left subtree size = inorder root index - inorder start.

title: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
brute: Store preorder traversal. Rebuild as right-skewed tree.
better: Recursive: flatten left, flatten right, attach.
optimal: Morris traversal approach: find rightmost node of left subtree, connect it to right subtree. Move left subtree to right. Repeat.
trick: For each node with left child: find rightmost of left subtree, attach original right subtree there, move left to right, set left=null. Process node by node top-down.

title: Kth Smallest and Largest element in BST
brute: Store inorder traversal in array. Return kth element.
optimal: Inorder traversal (left-root-right) of BST gives sorted order. Count nodes visited. Return when count == k. No extra array needed.
trick: BST inorder is sorted ascending. Kth smallest = kth in inorder. Kth largest = (n-k+1)th in inorder OR reverse inorder (right-root-left).

title: Correct BST with two nodes swapped
brute: Store inorder. Find two swapped elements. Swap values back.
optimal: Inorder traversal. Track prev node. If prev > current: violation found. First violation: first = prev, second = current. Second violation: update second only. Swap first and second values.
trick: Two swapped nodes cause 1 or 2 violations in inorder. Always update 'second' at each violation but update 'first' only at first violation. Then swap their values (not nodes).

title: Alien Dictionary
brute: Try all character orderings.
optimal: Build directed graph from adjacent word pairs. Compare char by char — first difference gives edge u->v (u comes before v). Topological sort gives alien alphabet order.
trick: Edge case: if word1 is prefix of word2 but appears after it in list — invalid (return ''). Build edges from consecutive word comparisons only. Cycle = invalid dictionary.

title: Number of ways to arrive at destination
brute: Find all paths, count those with minimum distance.
optimal: Dijkstra with ways[] array. dist[node] = shortest distance, ways[node] = number of ways to reach with shortest distance. If new shortest: update dist and ways. If equal: add ways.
trick: Extend Dijkstra: carry ways[] alongside dist[]. When relaxing edge: if shorter path found → update dist AND reset ways. If equal path → add to ways count. Modulo 1e9+7.

title: Preorder Traversal
brute: N/A
better: Recursive: visit root, recurse left, recurse right.
optimal: Iterative with stack: push root. While stack not empty: pop, add to result, push RIGHT then LEFT (so left processed first).
trick: Iterative: push right before left (stack is LIFO). Preorder used in serialization/deserialization and expression trees.

title: Inorder Traversal
brute: N/A
better: Recursive: recurse left, visit root, recurse right.
optimal: Iterative with stack: go as far left as possible pushing nodes. Pop, add to result, go right. OR Morris traversal: O(1) space.
trick: BST inorder = sorted array. Iterative: curr = root. While curr or stack: go left pushing all, pop, add, go right. Morris: use threaded links to avoid stack.

title: Postorder Traversal
brute: N/A
better: Recursive: recurse left, recurse right, visit root.
optimal: Two stack method: push root to stack1. Pop to stack2, push left then right to stack1. Reverse of stack2 = postorder. OR modify preorder (root, right, left) and reverse.
trick: Postorder = reverse of (root, right, left). Do modified preorder, reverse result. Used in deletion (delete children before parent) and expression evaluation.

title: Check for balanced binary tree
brute: For each node compute height of left and right subtrees. Check |left-right| <= 1. O(n²).
optimal: Single pass DFS: return -1 if unbalanced at any point. Otherwise return height. If |leftH - rightH| > 1: return -1. Else return 1 + max(leftH, rightH).
pattern: Tree DFS — return height or -1 for unbalanced
trick: Return height normally, but return -1 as sentinel for 'unbalanced'. Propagate -1 up — if either child returns -1, current node also returns -1.

title: Rearrange array elements by sign
brute: Separate into positives and negatives. Interleave.
optimal: Two write pointers: pos=0 (even indices), neg=1 (odd indices). Single pass: place positive at pos, pos+=2. Place negative at neg, neg+=2.
trick: Positives at even indices, negatives at odd indices. Fill simultaneously in one pass using two write pointers advancing by 2.

title: Longest subarray with sum K
brute: Check all subarrays. O(n²).
optimal: Prefix sum + HashMap: store first occurrence of each prefix sum. If prefixSum - K exists in map: length = i - map[prefixSum-K]. Track max length.
trick: Initialize map with {0: -1}. This handles subarrays starting at index 0. Don't overwrite existing entries — we want FIRST occurrence for longest subarray.

title: First and last occurrence
brute: Linear scan for first and last occurrence.
optimal: Two binary searches: one for first occurrence (lower bound) and one for last occurrence (upper bound - 1). First: when found, go left. Last: when found, go right.
pattern: Binary search — two passes for boundaries
trick: First occurrence: when arr[mid]==target, save and go left (high=mid-1). Last occurrence: when arr[mid]==target, save and go right (low=mid+1).

title: Count Occurrences in a Sorted Array
brute: Linear scan, count occurrences. O(n).
optimal: Find first and last occurrence using binary search. Count = last - first + 1. If not found, return 0.
trick: Directly build on First and Last Occurrence problem. count = upperBound(target) - lowerBound(target).

title: Search in rotated sorted array-II
brute: Linear search. O(n).
optimal: Like Search I but handle duplicates: if arr[low]==arr[mid]==arr[high]: low++, high-- (shrink both ends). Otherwise same as Search I.
pattern: Binary Search on rotated array with duplicates
trick: Duplicates at boundaries create ambiguity — can't determine which half is sorted. Solution: shrink both boundaries when arr[low]==arr[mid]==arr[high]. Worst case O(n).
similar: Search in rotated sorted array I

title: Find out how many times the array is rotated
brute: Find minimum element, return its index.
optimal: Binary search for minimum element index. That index = number of rotations. If arr[low] <= arr[high]: array sorted, min at low. Else find pivot.
trick: Rotation count = index of minimum element. Same as Find Minimum in Rotated Array but return index instead of value.

title: Find Nth root of a number
brute: Try all integers from 1 to m. Check if i^n == m.
optimal: Binary search on answer (1 to m). For mid: compute mid^n. If == m: found. If < m: go right. If > m: go left.
trick: Computing mid^n carefully to avoid overflow — use long or check during multiplication if exceeding m. Return -1 if not perfect nth root.

title: Minimum days to make M bouquets
brute: Try each day from min to max blooming day. Check if m bouquets possible.
optimal: Binary search on days (min to max in bloomDay). For each mid: count bouquets possible (consecutive bloomed flowers in groups of k). If >= m: try fewer days.
trick: Feasibility check: iterate through bloomDay, count consecutive flowers bloomed by day 'mid'. When k consecutive found, bouquet count++. Check if total >= m.

title: Find the smallest divisor
brute: Try all divisors from 1 to max(nums).
optimal: Binary search on divisor (1 to max(nums)). For each mid: sum = sum(ceil(num/mid)) for all nums. If sum <= threshold: try smaller divisor. Else try larger.
trick: ceil(a/b) = (a + b - 1) / b in integer arithmetic. Minimize divisor while keeping sum <= threshold.

title: Capacity to Ship Packages Within D Days
brute: Try all capacities from max(weights) to sum(weights).
optimal: Binary search on capacity (max(weights) to sum(weights)). For each mid capacity: greedily simulate days needed. If days <= D: try lower capacity.
trick: Lower bound = max single weight (must fit in ship). Upper bound = total weight (ship all in 1 day). Greedy check: fill ship until next package would exceed capacity, start new day.

title: Kth Missing Positive Number
brute: Count missing numbers one by one until kth missing found.
optimal: Binary search: missing numbers before arr[mid] = arr[mid] - (mid+1). If missing < k: go right. Else go left. Answer = low + k.
trick: Missing count before index mid = arr[mid] - (mid+1). When loop ends, low is first index where missing >= k. Answer = low + k (k missing numbers after accounting for array elements).

title: Middle of a LinkedList
brute: Count nodes, traverse to n/2.
optimal: Slow and fast pointers. slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. When fast reaches end, slow is at middle.
trick: For even length: fast.next == null gives second middle. fast == null gives first middle. Adjust condition based on requirement.

title: Rotate a LL
brute: Rotate one step at a time, k times.
optimal: 1) Find length and tail. 2) k = k % length (handle large k). 3) Find new tail at (length - k - 1). 4) New head = newTail.next. 5) Connect old tail to old head. 6) Cut at new tail.
pattern: Linked List — find split point and reconnect
trick: k % length handles when k > length. Make list circular, find new head position, cut. New tail is at position (length - k % length - 1) from start.
similar: Reverse LL in groups of K

title: Clone a LL with random and next pointer
brute: HashMap mapping original to copy. First pass: create all copies. Second pass: assign next and random using map.
optimal: No extra space: 1) Interleave copies between original nodes. 2) Set random pointers for copies. 3) Separate original and copy lists.
trick: HashMap approach is simpler and acceptable in interviews. Interleaving: insert copy after each original. copy.random = original.random.next. Then separate.

title: Subsets I
brute: N/A
optimal: Backtracking: at each index, two choices — include or exclude. Add current subset to result at every step (not just when done). OR bit manipulation: for i in 0 to 2^n-1, include element j if bit j is set.
trick: Add to result at EVERY recursive call, not just base case — that's what generates all subsets including empty set and full set. Total 2^n subsets.

title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
brute: N/A
optimal: Backtracking: for each digit, try all mapped letters. Recurse to next digit. Add to result when string length == digits length.
trick: Map digit to letters (phone keypad). At each level: iterate over letters for current digit, add to path, recurse. Undo after recursion. Base case: path length == digits length.

title: Palindrome Partitioning
brute: Generate all partitions. Check each part if palindrome.
optimal: Backtracking: at each start index, try all substrings ending at j. If palindrome: add to current partition, recurse from j+1. Add complete partition to result at end.
trick: Precompute isPalindrome[i][j] with DP for O(1) palindrome check during backtracking. isPalindrome[i][j] = (s[i]==s[j]) AND isPalindrome[i+1][j-1].

title: Remove Outermost Parentheses
brute: Find primitive decompositions manually.
optimal: Track depth with counter. When depth > 0: add char to result. Open bracket: depth++. Close bracket: depth--. Outermost brackets have depth 0 before opening and 1 before closing.
trick: Increment depth BEFORE adding open bracket check (depth > 0). Decrement AFTER adding close bracket check. Outermost ( has depth 0 before increment, outermost ) has depth 1 before decrement.
similar: Minimum Remove to Make Valid Parentheses

title: Largest Odd Number in a String
brute: Check all substrings, find largest odd one.
optimal: Traverse from RIGHT. Find rightmost odd digit. Return prefix up to and including that digit.
trick: A number is odd if its LAST digit is odd. So find rightmost odd digit, return everything up to it. Greedy: rightmost odd digit gives longest possible odd prefix.

title: Longest Common Prefix
brute: Compare all strings character by character. O(n*m).
optimal: Start with first string as prefix. For each subsequent string: shrink prefix until it matches string's start. Return prefix.
trick: Use String.startsWith() or index check. Shrink from right until match found. After all strings: remaining prefix is LCP. OR sort and compare only first and last strings.

title: Isomorphic String
brute: Try all character mappings.
optimal: Two HashMaps: s->t mapping and t->s mapping. For each pair: if mapping exists but conflicts: return false. If doesn't exist: add mapping. Check BOTH directions.
trick: Must check BOTH directions — s->t AND t->s. One-way mapping misses cases like s='aa', t='ab'. Both chars must map consistently to each other.

title: Check if two strings are anagram
brute: Sort both strings, compare.
optimal: Count frequency of each char in s, decrement for each char in t. If any count != 0: not anagram. OR single int array of size 26.
trick: Single array[26]: increment for s chars, decrement for t chars. If any non-zero at end: not anagram. Early exit if lengths differ.

title: Sort Characters by Frequency
brute: Count frequencies. Sort characters by frequency. Build string.
optimal: HashMap for frequencies. Sort by frequency descending. OR bucket sort: array of lists indexed by frequency. Collect from high to low frequency.
time_complexity: O(n log n) sort, O(n) bucket
trick: Bucket sort: index = frequency, value = list of chars with that frequency. Max frequency = n. Collect from bucket n down to 1.

title: Roman to Integer
brute: Handle all special cases (IV, IX, etc.) separately.
optimal: Traverse right to left. If current value < previous value: subtract. Else add. Map each symbol to value.
trick: Right-to-left traversal: if current < right neighbor, subtract current value (handles IV, IX, XL etc). Otherwise add. Elegant — no special case handling.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
brute: Check all substrings if palindrome. O(n³).
better: DP: dp[i][j] = is s[i..j] palindrome. O(n²) time and space.
optimal: Expand around center: for each char (and between chars): expand outward while palindrome. Track max. O(n²) time O(1) space. OR Manacher's O(n).
trick: 2n-1 centers (n chars + n-1 between chars). Expand around each center. Manacher's is O(n) but complex for interviews — expand around center usually sufficient.

title: Rotate String
brute: Try all rotations, check if any equals goal.
optimal: s + s contains all rotations of s. Check if goal is substring of s + s. Also check len(s) == len(goal).
pattern: String — rotation = substring of doubled string
trick: Key insight: s + s contains ALL rotations of s. So goal is a rotation iff goal in (s+s) and same length. Use contains() or KMP.

title: House robber II
brute: Try all subsets of non-adjacent houses in circular arrangement.
optimal: Circular = can't take both first and last. Run House Robber I twice: once on nums[0..n-2], once on nums[1..n-1]. Return max of both.
trick: Break circular constraint by fixing one endpoint. If we exclude last house: rob from 0 to n-2. If we exclude first: rob from 1 to n-1. Max of both = answer.

title: Frog Jump
brute: Try all jump possibilities recursively.
optimal: dp[i] = minimum energy to reach stone i. dp[i] = min(dp[i-1] + |h[i]-h[i-1]|, dp[i-2] + |h[i]-h[i-2]|). Base: dp[0]=0.
trick: Can jump 1 or 2 steps. dp[i] depends on dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] — same structure as Fibonacci/Climbing Stairs but with cost.

title: Longest common substring
brute: Check all substrings of both strings for matches.
optimal: dp[i][j] = length of longest common substring ending at s1[i] and s2[j]. If s1[i]==s2[j]: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]+1. Else 0. Track global max.
pattern: DP on strings — reset on mismatch
trick: Unlike LCS: reset to 0 on mismatch (substring must be contiguous). Can optimize space to O(min(m,n)) using 1D DP.

title: Target sum
brute: Try all +/- assignments recursively. O(2^n).
optimal: Convert to subset sum: assign + to subset P, - to subset N. P - N = target AND P + N = sum. So P = (sum + target) / 2. Count subsets with sum P.
trick: Mathematical reduction: finding subsets to add vs subtract = finding subset with specific sum. Count DP: dp[j] += dp[j - nums[i]]. Iterate j backwards.

title: Coin Change 2
brute: Try all combinations of coins. Count those summing to amount.
optimal: Unbounded knapsack. dp[j] = number of ways to make amount j. For each coin: for j from coin to amount: dp[j] += dp[j-coin]. dp[0]=1.
trick: Outer loop = coins (ensures each combination counted once, not permutations). Inner loop = amounts. Contrast with Coin Change I which minimizes count. dp[0]=1 as base.

title: Wildcard matching
brute: Recursive with all possibilities for * and ?.
optimal: dp[i][j] = does p[0..j] match s[0..i]. If p[j]=='*': dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (use * for one char) OR dp[i][j-1] (use * for empty). If p[j]=='?' or match: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1].
trick: * can match empty (dp[i][j-1]) or any char (dp[i-1][j]). ? matches exactly one char. Initialize dp[0][0]=true. First row: dp[0][j] = p[0..j] all stars.

title: Best time to buy and sell stock II
brute: Try all buy/sell combinations.
optimal: Greedy: add all positive differences. If prices[i] > prices[i-1]: profit += prices[i] - prices[i-1]. Equivalent to buying and selling every day with profit.
trick: Unlimited transactions = capture every upward price movement. Sum of all positive daily differences = maximum profit. Equivalent to optimal buy-sell sequence.

title: Longest String Chain
brute: Try all orderings of words as chain.
optimal: Sort by length. dp[word] = longest chain ending with word. For each word: try removing each char to get predecessor. dp[word] = max(dp[predecessor]+1).
trick: Sort by word length. Predecessor = word with one char removed (try all positions). Use HashMap for dp. O(L²) per word: L choices to remove, L to form predecessor string.

title: Matrix chain multiplication
brute: Try all ways to parenthesize. Exponential.
optimal: Interval DP: dp[i][j] = min cost to multiply matrices from i to j. Try all split points k: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + dims[i-1]*dims[k]*dims[j]).
pattern: Interval DP — split at every point
trick: Fill by increasing interval length. Base: dp[i][i]=0. Cost of multiplying A(p×q) * B(q×r) = p*q*r. Split point k determines which multiplication happens last.

title: Palindrome Partitioning II
brute: Generate all partitions. Count min cuts for palindrome partition.
optimal: Precompute isPalin[i][j]. dp[i] = min cuts for s[0..i]. For each i: try all j from 0 to i: if s[j..i] is palindrome: dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[j-1]+1).
pattern: DP — minimum cuts with palindrome precomputation
trick: Precompute all palindrome substrings in O(n²). Then dp[i] = minimum cuts. If entire s[0..i] is palindrome: dp[i] = 0 (no cuts needed).

title: Shortest path in Undirected Graph
brute: DFS trying all paths, track minimum distance.
optimal: BFS from source. BFS naturally finds shortest path in unweighted graph. dist[] array initialized to -1. When node visited: dist[node] = dist[parent] + 1.
trick: BFS = shortest path for UNWEIGHTED graphs. Each BFS level = one more edge. Use Dijkstra for weighted graphs. dist[] starts as -1 (unvisited) not infinity.

title: Shortest path in DAG
brute: Try all paths using DFS.
optimal: Topological sort first. Then relax edges in topological order. dist[src]=0, rest=infinity. For each node in topo order: relax all outgoing edges.
trick: DAG has no cycles so Bellman-Ford overkill. Topo sort gives processing order that ensures all predecessors processed before current node. Single pass suffices.

title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
brute: Bellman-Ford: relax all edges V-1 times. O(VE).
optimal: Min-heap priority queue. Start with (0, src). Pop minimum distance node. For each neighbor: if dist[src]+weight < dist[neighbor]: update and push to heap.
pattern: Greedy + Min Heap — shortest path weighted graph
trick: Lazy deletion: don't remove stale entries from heap — just skip if dist[node] < popped distance. Only works for non-negative weights. Use for non-negative weighted shortest path.

title: Bellman Ford Algorithm
brute: N/A
optimal: Relax ALL edges V-1 times. dist[src]=0, rest=infinity. After V-1 iterations: if still able to relax an edge: negative cycle exists.
pattern: DP on edges — handles negative weights
trick: Why V-1? Shortest path has at most V-1 edges. Vth relaxation detects negative cycle. Use when graph has negative weights (Dijkstra fails). Works on directed graphs.

title: Floyd Warshall Algorithm
brute: Run Dijkstra from every node. O(V * (V+E) log V).
optimal: Triple nested loop: for k (intermediate): for i (source): for j (dest): dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]).
pattern: DP on all pairs — intermediate node enumeration
trick: k is the intermediate node — enumerate all possible intermediates. Initialize dist[i][i]=0, dist[i][j]=edge weight or infinity. Detects negative cycles: dist[i][i] < 0.

title: Prim's MST Algorithm
brute: Try all spanning trees, find minimum weight.
optimal: Greedy with min-heap. Start from any node. Always pick minimum weight edge connecting visited to unvisited node. Add to MST. Repeat V-1 times.
pattern: Greedy — grow MST from single node
trick: Similar to Dijkstra but key is edge weight not total path distance. Use visited[] array. Push all edges of newly added node to heap. Pick minimum unvisited.

title: Kruskal's MST Algorithm
brute: Try all spanning trees.
optimal: Sort all edges by weight. Use Disjoint Set Union (DSU). For each edge: if endpoints in different components: add to MST (union them). Stop when V-1 edges added.
pattern: Greedy + DSU — add minimum edges without cycle
trick: Sort edges, greedily add minimum weight edges that don't form cycle. DSU detects cycles efficiently: if find(u)==find(v), adding edge u-v creates cycle.

title: Disjoint Set Union (DSU)
brute: DFS/BFS to check connectivity.
optimal: Find with path compression: while node != parent[node]: parent[node]=parent[parent[node]], node=parent[node]. Union by rank: attach smaller tree under larger.
trick: Two optimizations: path compression (flatten tree during find) + union by rank/size (keep tree shallow). Together give near O(1) per operation. Essential for Kruskal's and connectivity problems.

title: Number of Islands II
brute: After each addition, DFS to count islands.
optimal: DSU approach. For each land addition: create new component. Check 4 neighbors — if already land and different component: union them, decrease island count. Track count dynamically.
trick: DSU perfect for dynamic problems where connectivity changes. On each land addition: islands++ then check neighbors. For each neighbor that's land and different component: islands-- and union.

title: Accounts Merge
brute: Compare all pairs of accounts, merge if common email.
optimal: DSU on emails. For each account: union all emails with first email (representative). Then group emails by their root. Sort each group, prepend account name.
trick: Email is the key, not account name. Map each email to account index for DSU. After unioning: group emails by find(email). HashMap: root_email -> [all emails in component].

title: Most Stones Removed with Same Row or Column
brute: Try all removal orders.
optimal: DSU: union stones sharing same row or column. Answer = total stones - number of connected components (one stone must remain per component).
pattern: DSU — stones in same row/col are connected
trick: Key insight: all stones in a connected component can be reduced to 1. So answer = n - components. Trick: union by row AND col: use offset for col (col + 10001) to avoid conflicts.

title: Making a Large Island
brute: For each 0: flip to 1, DFS to find island size. Track max.
optimal: DSU: assign each island a color/id. Store size of each island. For each 0 cell: sum sizes of distinct neighboring islands + 1. Track max.
pattern: DSU — precompute island sizes, query on flip
trick: Two passes: 1) Build DSU, compute island sizes. 2) For each 0: check 4 neighbors' island ids (use set to avoid duplicates), sum their sizes + 1. Max = answer.

title: Swim in Rising Water
brute: Try all time values, check if path exists using DFS.
better: Binary search on time + BFS/DFS check.
optimal: Dijkstra variant: treat cell value as cost. Min-heap: (max_time_so_far, row, col). Extract min, expand to neighbors with max(current_time, neighbor_value). Reach (n-1,n-1) = answer.
trick: Instead of sum of weights, minimize the MAXIMUM value along path. dist[cell] = minimum possible maximum time to reach cell. Dijkstra works because we always process minimum time first.

title: Bridges in Graph
brute: Remove each edge, check if graph becomes disconnected. O(E*(V+E)).
optimal: Tarjan's algorithm: DFS with discovery time and low[] values. low[v] = min discovery time reachable from subtree of v. Edge u-v is bridge if low[v] > disc[u].
pattern: Tarjan's bridge finding — DFS with low values
trick: low[v] = minimum of disc[v], disc[neighbors], low[children in DFS tree]. Bridge condition: low[v] > disc[u] means v can't reach u or ancestors without u-v edge.

title: Articulation Point
brute: Remove each vertex, check if graph becomes disconnected. O(V*(V+E)).
optimal: Tarjan's: DFS with disc[] and low[]. Node u is articulation point if: 1) root of DFS tree with 2+ children, OR 2) non-root with child v where low[v] >= disc[u].
trick: Two conditions: root case (2+ children in DFS tree) and non-root case (low[child] >= disc[node]). low[v] >= disc[u] means v can't bypass u to reach u's ancestors.

title: Strongly Connected Components (Kosaraju)
brute: For each node: DFS on original and reversed graph. Check mutual reachability.
optimal: Kosaraju's: 1) DFS on original, push to stack in finish order. 2) Transpose graph. 3) DFS from stack top on transposed graph — each DFS gives one SCC.
pattern: Two DFS on original and transposed graph
trick: Finish order in step 1 ensures we start SCCs from the right nodes. Transposing reverses reachability. Nodes reachable in transposed from a node = its SCC.

title: Maximum Points You Can Obtain from Cards
brute: Try all combinations of k cards from front and back.
