optimal: Take k cards total from both ends. Equivalent: find minimum sum subarray of length (n-k) in the middle. Answer = totalSum - minMiddleSum.
trick: Instead of maximizing cards taken: minimize the cards NOT taken (n-k consecutive cards in middle). Fixed window of size n-k sliding through array.

title: Task Scheduler
brute: Simulate all possible schedules.
optimal: Math approach: most frequent task determines minimum time. slots = (maxFreq-1) * (n+1) + countOfMaxFreq. Answer = max(slots, total tasks).
trick: Most frequent task creates 'frames'. Each frame has n+1 slots. Last frame may be incomplete. If enough tasks to fill all idle slots: no idle time needed. Answer = max(formula, len(tasks)).

title: Fractional Knapsack
brute: Try all combinations of items.
optimal: Calculate value/weight ratio for each item. Sort by ratio descending. Greedily take items with highest ratio. Take fraction of last item if needed.
trick: Unlike 0/1 knapsack, fractions allowed so greedy works. Sort by value/weight ratio. Take as much of highest ratio item as possible. Greedy gives optimal here.

title: Candy
brute: Try all candy distributions.
optimal: Two passes: Left to right — if ratings[i] > ratings[i-1]: candies[i] = candies[i-1]+1. Right to left — if ratings[i] > ratings[i+1]: candies[i] = max(candies[i], candies[i+1]+1). Sum all.
pattern: Greedy — two pass left and right
trick: Left pass ensures left neighbor satisfied. Right pass ensures right neighbor satisfied. Take max of both passes for each position. Both constraints satisfied simultaneously.

title: Job Sequencing Problem
brute: Try all job orderings.
optimal: Sort jobs by profit descending. For each job: find latest available slot <= deadline. Schedule there. Use DSU for efficient slot finding.
time_complexity: O(n log n + n*d) naive, O(n log n) with DSU
pattern: Greedy — highest profit first with deadline constraint
trick: Always try to schedule at the LATEST possible slot (deadline or earlier free slot) to keep earlier slots free for other jobs. DSU finds next available slot efficiently.

title: Shortest Job First (CPU Scheduling)
brute: Try all scheduling orders.
optimal: Sort by arrival time. Use min-heap by burst time. At each time unit: add all arrived jobs to heap. Process shortest job. Track waiting times.
trick: Preemptive SJF: at each moment pick shortest remaining time. Non-preemptive: once started, run to completion. Min-heap maintains available jobs sorted by burst time.

title: Check if Array is Sorted II
brute: Check all rotations if sorted.
optimal: Count inversions (arr[i] > arr[i+1]). Include wraparound: arr[n-1] > arr[0]. If inversions <= 1: sorted and rotated (or just sorted).
trick: A sorted+rotated array has exactly 0 or 1 'drop' in value. Count drops including wraparound. If count <= 1: yes. If 0: not rotated. If 1: rotated once.

title: Find missing number in array
brute: Sort, scan for missing. Or HashSet approach.
optimal: Expected sum = n*(n+1)/2. Missing = expectedSum - actualSum. OR XOR: XOR all indices 0..n with all values — missing number remains.
trick: XOR trick avoids overflow: XOR all i from 0 to n, XOR all arr[i]. Paired elements cancel. Missing number remains. Sum trick: simple but may overflow for large n (use long).
similar: Find all numbers disappeared in array

title: Candy Distribution
brute: Simulate giving candies based on neighbors.
optimal: Two arrays: left[] and right[]. left[i] = candies considering only left neighbor. right[i] = candies considering only right neighbor. Answer = sum of max(left[i], right[i]).
trick: Equivalent to Candy problem above. Left sweep: if ratings[i]>ratings[i-1]: left[i]=left[i-1]+1. Right sweep similarly. Take max at each position.

title: Count Subarrays with K Different Integers
brute: Check all subarrays, count distinct elements.
optimal: exactly K = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1). atMost(K): sliding window with HashMap counting frequencies. Shrink when distinct > K. Count = right - left + 1 at each step.
pattern: Sliding Window — exactly K = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1)
trick: Counting exactly K is hard directly. Count at most K - at most K-1 = exactly K. This trick applies to many 'exactly K' problems.
similar: Longest Substring with at most K distinct

title: Find Kth element in two sorted arrays
brute: Merge both arrays, return kth element.
better: Two pointer merge up to kth element.
optimal: Binary search: eliminate k/2 elements at a time. Compare arr1[k/2-1] with arr2[k/2-1]. Eliminate smaller half (those can't be kth element). Reduce k by eliminated count.
trick: At each step: compare k/2 th element of both arrays. Smaller side's first k/2 elements can't be kth smallest (something from other array is smaller). Eliminate and reduce k.

title: Search in 2D matrix II
brute: Linear search entire matrix. O(m*n).
better: Binary search each row. O(m log n).
optimal: Start from top-right corner. If target == element: found. If target < element: move left. If target > element: move down.
trick: Top-right has property: everything to left is smaller, everything below is larger. This gives clear elimination direction. Start top-right (or bottom-left), not top-left or bottom-right.

title: Find Peak Element II
brute: Check all elements, find local maximum.
optimal: Binary search on columns. For mid column: find row with maximum value. Check left and right neighbors. If peak: return. If left neighbor larger: go left half. Else go right half.
pattern: Binary Search on 2D — column binary search
trick: Max in column guarantees we can find peak. If max of mid column is less than its left neighbor: peak must exist in left half (can prove by contradiction). Same logic for right.

title: Merge K sorted arrays
brute: Collect all elements, sort them. O(n*k log(n*k)).
better: Merge arrays two at a time. O(nk²).
optimal: Min-heap of size k. Initialize with first element of each array. Extract min, add to result, push next element from same array.
trick: Store (value, arrayIndex, elementIndex) in heap. On extraction: push next element from same array. Same pattern as Merge K Sorted Lists.
similar: Find Kth Smallest in Sorted Matrix

title: Find Median in a stream
brute: Sort array each time, find median.
better: Insert in sorted position, find median.
optimal: Two heaps: maxHeap (lower half), minHeap (upper half). Balance: maxHeap.size = minHeap.size OR maxHeap.size = minHeap.size + 1. Median = maxHeap.top() or average of both tops.
trick: Always add to maxHeap first. If maxHeap.top > minHeap.top: rebalance. If sizes differ by 2: move top to other heap. Median always accessible in O(1).

title: Two Sum II - sorted array
brute: Check all pairs. O(n²).
better: Binary search for complement. O(n log n).
optimal: Two pointers: left=0, right=n-1. If sum==target: return. If sum<target: left++. If sum>target: right--.
trick: Works because array is sorted. Moving left increases sum, moving right decreases sum. Guaranteed to find if exists.

title: Bit Manipulation Basics
brute: N/A
optimal: Key operations: AND (&), OR (|), XOR (^), NOT (~), Left shift (<<), Right shift (>>). Check bit i: (n >> i) & 1. Set bit i: n | (1 << i). Clear bit i: n & ~(1 << i).
trick: n & (n-1) removes lowest set bit. n & (-n) isolates lowest set bit. XOR: a^a=0, a^0=a. Use for toggling, checking parity, finding unique elements.

title: Count number of bits to flip
brute: Convert both to binary, compare digit by digit.
optimal: XOR the two numbers. Count set bits in result (different bits = 1 in XOR). Count set bits: while n>0: count += n&1, n>>=1. OR use n&(n-1) to clear lowest bit.
pattern: Bit manipulation — XOR then count bits
trick: XOR gives 1 where bits differ. Count 1s in XOR result = number of flips needed. Brian Kernighan: n & (n-1) removes lowest set bit in O(set bits) time.

title: Power of 2
brute: Divide by 2 repeatedly, check if reaches 1.
optimal: Power of 2 has exactly one bit set. Check: n > 0 AND (n & (n-1)) == 0.
trick: n & (n-1) clears lowest set bit. If result is 0: only one bit was set = power of 2. n=0 is NOT power of 2, handle separately.

title: Minimum Bit Flips to Convert Number
brute: Compare binary representations.
optimal: XOR start and goal. Count set bits in result using Brian Kernighan's algorithm.
trick: Same as 'count bits to flip'. XOR highlights differences, count 1s = minimum flips.

title: Count set bits from 1 to N
brute: For each number 1 to N, count its set bits. Sum all. O(n log n).
optimal: For each bit position i: complete cycles = n / (2^(i+1)). Each cycle contributes 2^i ones. Remaining = max(0, (n % 2^(i+1)) - 2^i + 1). Sum all bit positions.
trick: Bit i follows pattern: 2^i zeros then 2^i ones repeating. Count complete patterns and partial last pattern. Alternatively: dp[i] = dp[i>>1] + (i&1).
similar: Count set bits in single number

title: Reverse Bits
brute: Convert to binary string, reverse, convert back.
optimal: For 32 bits: result=0. For i in 0..31: result = (result << 1) | (n & 1), n >>= 1.
pattern: Bit manipulation — bit by bit construction
trick: Extract LSB with n&1. Shift result left to make room. OR in extracted bit. Shift n right. Repeat 32 times. Fixed number of iterations so O(1).

title: Find Two Non-Repeating Elements
brute: HashMap frequency count. Return two with count 1.
optimal: XOR all elements: result = a XOR b. Find any set bit (rightmost: result & -result). Partition elements by this bit. XOR each partition separately gives a and b.
trick: a XOR b has set bits where a and b differ. Use any set bit to separate a and b into different groups. Each group also contains other pairs which cancel out via XOR.

title: Divide Two Integers Without Division
brute: Subtract divisor repeatedly. O(quotient).
optimal: Exponential search: double the divisor using shifts until exceeds dividend. Subtract and count how many times. Repeat with remainder.
trick: Instead of subtracting once: subtract 2^k * divisor at once (where 2^k*divisor <= dividend). This is bit shifting. Handle overflow: use long. Handle signs separately.

title: Combination Sum III
brute: Try all subsets of size k.
optimal: Backtracking: digits 1-9, each used once. If k numbers summing to n found: add to result. Prune: remaining sum < 0 or remaining count < 0.
trick: Digits 1-9, no repetition, find k numbers summing to n. Start from previous digit+1 to avoid duplicates. Two pruning conditions: count and sum.

title: Word Search
brute: Try all starting positions and all paths.
optimal: Backtracking from each cell. If board[r][c] == word[index]: mark visited, recurse 4 neighbors with index+1. If all chars matched: return true. Undo marking (backtrack).
trick: Mark cell as visited by temporarily changing its value (e.g., to '#'). Restore after recursion. Avoids using extra visited array.

title: Generate Parentheses
brute: Generate all 2^(2n) strings of brackets, filter valid ones.
optimal: Backtracking with open and close counts. If open < n: add '(' and recurse. If close < open: add ')' and recurse. If both == n: add to result.
trick: Two rules: can add '(' if open < n. Can add ')' if close < open. These two rules guarantee all sequences valid. No need to validate at end.

title: Flood fill algorithm
brute: N/A
optimal: DFS from source. If cell == originalColor: change to newColor, recurse 4 neighbors. Base case: out of bounds or wrong color.
pattern: DFS/BFS on grid — paint connected region
trick: Handle edge case: if newColor == originalColor, return immediately to avoid infinite loop. In-place modification marks visited cells.

title: Surrounded Regions
brute: Find all enclosed regions directly.
optimal: Reverse thinking: mark all 'O's connected to border as safe (DFS/BFS from border 'O's). Then flip remaining 'O's to 'X' and restore safe ones to 'O'.
trick: Don't find enclosed regions directly (hard). Instead mark UNenclosed (border-connected) regions first. Everything else is enclosed. Classic reverse thinking pattern.

title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
brute: From each cell: DFS to check if reaches both oceans.
optimal: BFS/DFS from both ocean borders inward (reverse flow). Mark cells reachable from Pacific, mark cells reachable from Atlantic. Answer = intersection.
trick: Reverse the flow: instead of water flowing DOWN, mark cells that can flow UP to ocean. BFS from ocean borders going uphill (>= current height). Intersection = cells reaching both.

title: Detect cycle in directed graph
brute: Try all paths, check for repetition.
optimal: DFS with color states: WHITE(0)=unvisited, GRAY(1)=in current path, BLACK(2)=fully processed. If reach GRAY node: cycle found. OR Kahn's topo sort: if not all nodes processed = cycle.
trick: Key: GRAY = currently being explored in this DFS path. If we reach a GRAY node: we have a back edge = cycle. BLACK = safe (no cycle through this node).

title: Detect cycle in undirected graph
brute: Try all paths.
optimal: DFS/BFS with parent tracking. If visited neighbor != parent: cycle exists. OR DSU: if two nodes being unioned already in same component: cycle found.
pattern: DFS with parent / DSU cycle detection
trick: Undirected: must track parent to avoid false positives (going back on same edge). If visited[neighbor] AND neighbor != parent: cycle. Different from directed graph approach.

title: Number of Enclaves
brute: For each land cell: DFS to check if reaches border.
optimal: DFS/BFS from all border land cells, mark as visited. Count remaining unvisited land cells — these are enclaves.
trick: Same reverse thinking as Surrounded Regions. Mark border-connected land, count remaining. Enclaves = land cells not reachable from any border.

title: Word Search II
brute: Run Word Search I for each word. O(words * m*n*4^L).
optimal: Build Trie from all words. DFS on board: if current path exists in Trie, continue. If Trie node marks end of word: add to result. Prune when path not in Trie.
pattern: Trie + DFS backtracking — simultaneous word search
trick: Trie allows searching all words simultaneously. At each cell: check if prefix exists in Trie (pruning). Remove found words from Trie to avoid duplicates. Mark visited during DFS.

title: XOR of numbers in range
brute: XOR all numbers from L to R.
optimal: XOR(L to R) = XOR(1 to R) XOR XOR(1 to L-1). XOR(1 to n) follows pattern based on n%4: if n%4==0: n, ==1: 1, ==2: n+1, ==3: 0.
trick: XOR from 1 to n cycles with period 4. Memorize: n%4: 0→n, 1→1, 2→n+1, 3→0. Range XOR = prefix(R) XOR prefix(L-1).

title: Check Armstrong Number
brute: N/A
optimal: Count digits n. Extract each digit. Sum of (digit^n) for all digits. If sum == original number: Armstrong.
trick: Example: 153 = 1³ + 5³ + 3³. Count digits first using log10. Then extract each digit with % 10 and / 10. Use pow() function carefully for precision.

title: Print 1 to N using Recursion
brute: N/A
optimal: Base case: if n==0 return. Recurse(n-1). Print n. OR: Print n first, then recurse(n+1) up to N.
trick: Print AFTER recursion call = prints 1 to N (head recursion gives N to 1). Print BEFORE = prints N to 1. Swap order to change output.

title: Sum of First N Numbers
brute: Iterative sum.
optimal: Recursive: if n==0 return 0. Return n + sum(n-1). OR math formula: n*(n+1)/2.
trick: Gauss formula: sum = n*(n+1)/2. Classic story: Gauss summed 1 to 100 instantly by pairing (1+100, 2+99...) = 50 pairs of 101 = 5050.

title: Check if String is Palindrome
brute: Reverse string, compare.
optimal: Two pointers: left=0, right=n-1. While left<right: if s[left]!=s[right]: not palindrome. left++, right--. OR recursive: check outer chars, recurse on inner.
trick: Valid Palindrome LC version: ignore non-alphanumeric, case-insensitive. Two pointer handles both in-place. Recursive: base case len<=1, check s[0]==s[-1] and recurse on s[1:-1].

title: Permutations of String
brute: N/A
optimal: Backtracking: swap arr[i] with arr[start], recurse with start+1, swap back. OR: use visited array, pick each unused char at each position.
trick: Swap-based: avoids extra space for current permutation. At each position: try all chars not yet used by swapping into current position. Must swap back after recursion.

title: Alien Dictionary
brute: Try all character orderings, find valid one.
optimal: Build directed graph from consecutive word pairs. Compare char by char — first mismatch gives edge (char1 before char2). Topological sort gives order. Handle edge cases.
trick: Edge cases: word2 is prefix of word1 but comes after = invalid. If cycle in graph = invalid. Only compare CONSECUTIVE words in the list.

title: Minimum Spanning Tree
brute: Try all spanning trees, find minimum weight.
optimal: Kruskal's (sort edges + DSU) or Prim's (min-heap + visited). Both give MST. Kruskal's better for sparse graphs, Prim's for dense.
time_complexity: O(E log E) Kruskal, O((V+E) log V) Prim
trick: MST properties: V-1 edges, no cycles, minimum total weight. Cut property: minimum weight edge crossing any cut belongs to MST. Cycle property: maximum weight edge in any cycle NOT in MST.

title: Number of ways to arrive at destination
brute: DFS trying all paths, count shortest ones.
optimal: Modified Dijkstra: maintain dist[] and ways[]. When shorter path found: update dist and reset ways. When equal path found: add ways. Answer = ways[destination].
trick: Two arrays: dist[] for minimum distance, ways[] for number of ways. Modulo 1e9+7 for ways count. When dist[v] == dist[u]+w: ways[v] += ways[u].

title: Print N to 1 using Recursion
brute: N/A
optimal: Print n BEFORE recursion: print(n), recurse(n-1). Base case: if n==0 return.
trick: Contrast with Print 1 to N: just swap print position relative to recursive call. Print before call = N to 1. Print after call = 1 to N.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence (DP)
brute: Generate all subsequences, check if increasing, find longest. O(2^n).
optimal: dp[i] = LIS ending at index i. For each i: for j<i: if arr[j]<arr[i]: dp[i]=max(dp[i], dp[j]+1). Answer = max(dp). O(n²). Binary search O(n log n) with tails array.
time_complexity: O(n²) DP, O(n log n) binary search
pattern: DP — LIS with binary search optimization
trick: Tails array: for each element, binary search for first tail >= element, replace it. Length of tails = LIS length. Doesn't give actual subsequence but gives length optimally.

title: Shortest Common Supersequence
brute: Generate all common supersequences, find shortest.
optimal: SCS length = m + n - LCS(s1, s2). To reconstruct: use LCS DP table. If chars match: take once. Else take from whichever string gives shorter SCS.
trick: SCS = merge both strings keeping common chars once. Length = m + n - LCS. Reconstruct by tracing LCS DP table: matching chars included once, non-matching chars from respective strings.

title: Distinct Subsequences
brute: Generate all subsequences of s, count those equal to t.
optimal: dp[i][j] = number of ways to form t[0..j] using s[0..i]. If s[i]==t[j]: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]+dp[i-1][j]. Else: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j].
trick: When chars match: either use s[i] to match t[j] (dp[i-1][j-1]) OR skip s[i] (dp[i-1][j]). When no match: must skip s[i]. Base: dp[i][0]=1 (empty t matched by any prefix of s).

title: Count Palindromic Substrings
brute: Check all substrings if palindrome. O(n³).
better: DP table: dp[i][j] = is s[i..j] palindrome. O(n²).
optimal: Expand around center: for each center (2n-1 centers), expand while palindrome, count each. O(n²) time O(1) space.
trick: 2n-1 centers: n single chars + n-1 between chars. Each expansion gives one palindrome. Count as you expand. Manacher's gives O(n) but complex.

title: Minimum Cost Climbing Stairs
brute: Try all paths recursively.
optimal: dp[i] = min cost to reach step i. dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Answer = min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]).
trick: Can start from index 0 or 1. dp[0]=cost[0], dp[1]=cost[1]. For i>=2: dp[i]=cost[i]+min(dp[i-1],dp[i-2]). Answer = min of last two (can reach top from either).

title: Triangle Path Sum
brute: Try all paths from top to bottom.
optimal: Bottom-up DP: start from second last row. For each element: dp[i][j] += min(dp[i+1][j], dp[i+1][j+1]). Answer = dp[0][0]. Or O(n) space using 1D array.
trick: Go bottom-up to avoid tracking choices. Each cell gets minimum of two children below it. 1D space: use last row as dp array, update in place going upward.

title: Grid Unique Paths
brute: Recursion: paths(m,n) = paths(m-1,n) + paths(m,n-1). Exponential.
optimal: dp[i][j] = unique paths to cell (i,j). dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. Base: first row and col = 1. OR combinatorics: C(m+n-2, m-1).
trick: Combinatorics: total moves = m+n-2. Must choose m-1 down moves. Answer = C(m+n-2, m-1). But DP is more intuitive and generalizes to obstacles.

title: Grid Unique Paths II (obstacles)
brute: Recursion trying all paths, skip obstacles.
optimal: Same as Unique Paths but set dp[i][j]=0 if obstacle. If start or end is obstacle: return 0.
trick: Only difference from Unique Paths: if grid[i][j]==1 (obstacle): dp[i][j]=0. Handle edge case: if grid[0][0] or grid[m-1][n-1] is obstacle, return 0.

title: Minimum Path Sum
brute: Try all paths from top-left to bottom-right.
optimal: dp[i][j] = minimum path sum to reach (i,j). dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Handle boundary (first row, first col).
trick: Can modify grid in-place to save space. dp[i][j] built from left and top neighbors. Only right and down moves allowed.

title: Cherry Pickup II
brute: Try all combinations of two paths from top to bottom.
optimal: 3D DP: dp[r][c1][c2] = max cherries when robot1 at col c1 and robot2 at col c2 in row r. Both move simultaneously row by row. Try all 9 combinations of moves.
trick: Both robots always in same row (move simultaneously). State = (row, col1, col2). Only need row in state since they move together. 9 next states (3 moves each). If c1==c2: count cherry once.

title: Subset Sum Problem
brute: Try all subsets, check if any sums to target.
optimal: dp[i][j] = can we achieve sum j using first i elements. dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] OR (j>=arr[i] AND dp[i-1][j-arr[i]]). Space optimize to 1D.
trick: 1D DP: iterate j from sum DOWN to arr[i]. This ensures each element used at most once. dp[0]=true (empty subset). Fill left to right for unbounded, right to left for 0/1.

title: Count Subsets with Sum K
brute: Try all subsets, count those summing to K.
optimal: dp[j] = number of subsets with sum j. For each element: for j from target down to arr[i]: dp[j] += dp[j-arr[i]]. dp[0]=1.
trick: Extension of Subset Sum. Instead of boolean, count ways. Handle zeros carefully: a zero can either be included or not — doubles the count. dp[0]=1 as base.

title: 0/1 Knapsack
brute: Try all subsets of items, find max value within weight.
optimal: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with capacity w. If weight[i]>w: dp[i][w]=dp[i-1][w]. Else: dp[i][w]=max(dp[i-1][w], val[i]+dp[i-1][w-weight[i]]).
trick: 1D optimization: iterate weight BACKWARDS to avoid using item twice. dp[w] = max(dp[w], val[i]+dp[w-weight[i]]). Template for many subsequence DP problems.

title: Unbounded Knapsack
brute: Try all combinations with repetition.
optimal: dp[w] = max value with capacity w. For each item: for w from weight[i] to W (FORWARD): dp[w] = max(dp[w], val[i]+dp[w-weight[i]]).
trick: Key difference from 0/1: iterate weight FORWARDS (allows reuse). Same item can contribute multiple times. Template for Coin Change, Rod Cutting.

title: Rod Cutting Problem
brute: Try all ways to cut rod, find max revenue.
optimal: Unbounded Knapsack variant. dp[i] = max revenue from rod of length i. For each cut length j (1 to i): dp[i] = max(dp[i], price[j] + dp[i-j]).
trick: Rod length = knapsack capacity. Each cut length = item. Price = value. Items can repeat (unbounded). Classic unbounded knapsack application.

title: Print Shortest Path in Weighted Graph
brute: DFS all paths, track minimum.
optimal: Dijkstra with parent[] array tracking previous node on shortest path. After running Dijkstra: backtrack from destination to source using parent[] to reconstruct path.
trick: Track parent[v] = u when dist[v] is updated via u. After algorithm: start from destination, follow parent pointers to source. Reverse the path.

title: Path with Maximum Probability
brute: DFS all paths, compute probability, find max.
optimal: Modified Dijkstra: maximize probability instead of minimizing distance. Use max-heap. Prob of path = product of edge probabilities. Update if new path has higher probability.
trick: Change min-heap to max-heap, change + to *. Probability is product of edges (not sum). Works because probabilities 0-1 so product is monotonically non-increasing.

title: Eventual Safe States
brute: DFS from each node, check if reaches cycle.
optimal: Reverse graph edges. Terminal nodes (outdegree 0 in original) have indegree 0 in reversed. Run Kahn's topo sort on reversed graph. Nodes in topo order are safe.
trick: Safe node = not on cycle AND not leads to cycle. Reverse edges: safe nodes in original = nodes that can reach terminal nodes. Kahn's on reversed graph finds all safe nodes.

title: Find City with Smallest Number of Reachable Cities
brute: From each city: BFS/DFS to find reachable within threshold.
optimal: Floyd-Warshall to find all-pairs shortest paths. For each city: count cities reachable within distance threshold. Return city with fewest (highest index if tie).
trick: All-pairs shortest path needed since query is about all cities. Floyd-Warshall perfect here. After computing dist[][], count for each source how many dest have dist <= threshold.

title: Minimum Multiplications to reach End
brute: BFS/DFS trying all multiplications.
optimal: BFS (unweighted shortest path). State = current number mod 100000. For each state: try multiplying by each element in arr. If reaches end: return steps.
pattern: BFS on implicit graph — state space
trick: States are numbers mod 100000. BFS finds minimum multiplications. Similar to Word Ladder — implicit graph where edges are multiplications. Mark visited states.

title: Number of Distinct Islands
brute: Find all islands. Compare shapes pairwise.
optimal: DFS on each island, record path as string (direction taken). Normalize by subtracting base position. Add path string to HashSet. Size of set = distinct islands.
trick: Encode island shape as DFS path string (U/D/L/R + backtrack markers). Two islands with same shape produce same path string. HashSet removes duplicates automatically.

title: Bipartite Check using BFS
brute: Try all 2-colorings.
optimal: BFS coloring: assign color 0 to start. For each neighbor: if uncolored assign opposite color. If colored same as current: not bipartite. Handle disconnected components.
trick: Bipartite = 2-colorable = no odd cycles. BFS naturally assigns levels as colors. If conflict in coloring: odd cycle exists = not bipartite. Check all components.

title: Prerequisite Tasks (Topo Sort)
brute: Try all orderings, find valid one.
optimal: Build directed graph from prerequisites. Kahn's algorithm: find all 0 in-degree nodes, process BFS. If all nodes processed: valid order exists. Else cycle = impossible.
trick: Same as Course Schedule. Build adjacency list and in-degree array. Kahn's BFS gives valid ordering. If remaining unprocessed nodes: cycle exists.

title: Network Delay Time
brute: BFS/DFS trying all signal paths.
optimal: Dijkstra from source node k. Find maximum distance in dist[] array (all nodes must receive signal). If any node unreachable (dist=infinity): return -1.
trick: Signal reaches all nodes at time = max(all shortest paths from k). If any node unreachable: -1. Classic Dijkstra application.

title: 01 Matrix
brute: For each 1 cell: BFS to find nearest 0. O(m*n*(m+n)).
optimal: Multi-source BFS from all 0 cells simultaneously. Add all 0 cells to queue with distance 0. BFS outward updating distances for 1 cells. First time a 1 cell is reached = shortest distance.
trick: Reverse thinking: BFS from all 0s simultaneously instead of BFS from each 1. This gives shortest distance to nearest 0 for all cells in O(m*n). Same as Rotten Oranges pattern.
similar: As Far from Land as Possible

title: Redundant Connection
brute: Try removing each edge, check if still connected.
optimal: DSU: for each edge, if both nodes already in same component: this edge is redundant (return it). Else union them.
trick: First edge that connects two already-connected nodes creates a cycle = redundant. Process edges in order, return first redundant edge found.

title: Minimum Spanning Tree Problems
brute: Try all spanning trees.
optimal: Kruskal or Prim depending on graph density. Kruskal: sort edges, DSU. Prim: min-heap from any node.
trick: MST applications: minimize cost to connect all nodes (network design, clustering). Key property: V-1 edges, no cycles, minimum total weight.
similar: Minimum Cost to Connect All Points

title: Minimum Cost to Connect All Points
brute: Try all spanning trees on complete graph.
optimal: Prim's on complete graph. For each unvisited point: track minimum Manhattan distance to any visited point. Add minimum each step. No need to build full adjacency list.
trick: Since graph is complete (all points connected): Prim's with O(n²) beats Kruskal's O(n² log n) (too many edges to sort). Track minDist[] to current MST for each unvisited node.

title: Word Break Problem
brute: Try all ways to segment string. Exponential.
optimal: dp[i] = can s[0..i-1] be segmented. dp[0]=true. For each i: for each j<i: if dp[j] AND s[j..i-1] in dict: dp[i]=true.
time_complexity: O(n² * L) where L=avg word length
trick: Think of it as reaching positions. dp[i] = can we reach position i. For each position, try all words ending there. HashSet for O(1) word lookup.

title: Longest Bitonic Subsequence
brute: Generate all subsequences, check if bitonic, find longest.
optimal: For each index: LIS ending at i (left[i]) and LDS starting at i (right[i]). LDS = LIS on reversed array. Answer = max(left[i]+right[i]-1) for all i.
trick: Bitonic = first increases then decreases. For each peak: left[i] = LIS length ending at i, right[i] = LDS length starting at i. Length through peak i = left[i]+right[i]-1.

title: Number of Longest Increasing Subsequences
brute: Generate all subsequences, count LIS ones.
optimal: Two arrays: dp[i]=LIS length ending at i, count[i]=number of LIS ending at i. For j<i: if arr[j]<arr[i]: if dp[j]+1>dp[i]: update dp and reset count. If equal: add count[j] to count[i].
trick: Maintain parallel count array. When new LIS length found: reset count. When equal length found: add to count. Answer = sum of count[i] where dp[i]==maxLIS.

title: Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
brute: Try all buy/sell combinations with cooldown constraint.
optimal: State machine: hold = max(hold, notHold-price). notHold = max(notHold, prevNotHold+price). prevNotHold = notHold (updated before). Answer = notHold.
trick: Three states: holding stock, not holding (can buy), cooldown. After selling: must wait one day. Track 'prevCash' for the cooldown state. Update order matters.

title: Buy and Sell Stock with Transaction Fee
brute: Try all combinations with fee applied.
optimal: Greedy/DP: hold = max(hold, cash-price). cash = max(cash, hold+price-fee). Same as Stock II but subtract fee on sell.
trick: Fee applied once per transaction (on sell). Simple modification of Stock II. Deduct fee when selling: cash = max(cash, hold+price-fee). Greedy still works.

title: Egg Drop Problem
brute: Try all floors for each egg drop. Exponential.
better: dp[eggs][floors] = min attempts. Binary search on dp values.
optimal: Reverse DP: dp[m][k] = max floors testable with m moves and k eggs. dp[m][k] = dp[m-1][k-1] + dp[m-1][k] + 1. Find min m where dp[m][k] >= n.
trick: Reverse: instead of 'min trials to check n floors with k eggs', ask 'max floors checkable with m trials and k eggs'. dp[m][k] = floors from going up + floors from going down + current floor.

title: Painter Partition Problem
brute: Try all ways to partition boards among painters.
optimal: Binary search on answer (max(boards) to sum(boards)). Greedy check: can k painters paint all boards with max time = mid? Same as Book Allocation / Split Array Largest Sum.
trick: Minimize maximum time = binary search pattern. Same as Book Allocation with boards instead of books and painters instead of students.

title: Largest Rectangle in Matrix
brute: Check all rectangles of 1s. O(m²n²).
better: For each row as base: compute histogram heights, find largest rectangle in histogram.
optimal: For each row: compute heights[] (0 if current cell is 0, else heights[j]+1). Apply Largest Rectangle in Histogram. O(m*n).
pattern: Reduce to Largest Rectangle in Histogram
trick: Build cumulative histogram row by row. If matrix[i][j]=='1': height[j]++. Else: height[j]=0. Run histogram algorithm on each row's heights.

title: Maximal Square
brute: Try all possible squares, find largest of 1s.
optimal: dp[i][j] = side length of largest square with bottom-right at (i,j). If matrix[i][j]=='1': dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1. Track max.
trick: dp[i][j] = min of three neighbors + 1. Intuition: limited by smallest square among top, left, and top-left. min() because all three must have enough 1s to form larger square.

title: Stock Buy Sell IV (at most K transactions)
brute: Try all combinations of at most k transactions.
optimal: dp[k][2] where 2 = holding or not. For each day and each transaction: dp[i][1]=max(dp[i][1], dp[i-1][0]-price). dp[i][0]=max(dp[i][0], dp[i-1][1]+price). Use k+1 transaction slots.
pattern: DP on stocks — generalized k transactions
trick: If k >= n/2: unlimited transactions (use Stock II solution). Otherwise: 2D DP with k states. Each transaction = one buy + one sell.

title: Print all LCS
brute: Generate all subsequences of both strings, find common longest ones.
optimal: Compute LCS DP table. Backtrack from dp[m][n]: if chars match — include in LCS, go diagonal. Else go in direction of larger value. When both equal: branch in both directions.
pattern: DP + backtracking to print all solutions
trick: When dp[i-1][j] == dp[i][j-1]: multiple LCS exist — branch both ways. Use set to avoid duplicates. Recursive backtracking on DP table.

title: Russian Doll Envelopes
brute: Try all orderings of envelope nesting.
optimal: Sort by width ascending, height DESCENDING (for equal widths). Apply LIS on heights only. O(n log n) with binary search.
trick: Sort width ascending, height DESCENDING for equal widths. This ensures same-width envelopes can't contain each other in LIS. Then LIS on heights = answer.

title: Check if preorder is valid BST
brute: Reconstruct BST from preorder, verify.
optimal: Simulate BST construction using stack and lower bound. Process preorder: if current < lower_bound: invalid. If current > stack top: pop (updating lower bound), push current.
trick: Monotonic decreasing stack mimics going down left spine of BST. When we go right (larger value): pop elements, update lower bound. If any element < lower bound: invalid.

title: Design a Stack with increment operation
brute: Normal stack. For increment: iterate bottom k elements.
optimal: Lazy propagation: inc[] array stores pending increments. On increment(k, val): inc[min(k,size)-1] += val. On pop: add inc[top] to value, carry inc[top] to inc[top-1], reset inc[top].
trick: Don't actually increment all k elements. Store increment lazily at index k-1. When popping: add accumulated increment and propagate to element below.

title: The Celebrity Problem
brute: For each person: check if known by all and knows nobody. O(n²).
better: Graph approach: find node with in-degree n-1 and out-degree 0.
optimal: Two pointer or stack: eliminate non-celebrities. If A knows B: A can't be celebrity, eliminate A. If A doesn't know B: B can't be celebrity, eliminate B. Verify remaining candidate.
trick: Every knows() call eliminates one candidate. n-1 calls narrow to one candidate. Then verify in O(n). Stack: push all, pop two, eliminate one, push other. Verify last.

title: Implement Queue using Stack
brute: Two stacks. On enqueue: push to stack1. On dequeue: move all to stack2, pop.
optimal: Amortized O(1): Push to stack1. Pop from stack2. If stack2 empty: move all from stack1 to stack2. Each element moved at most once total.
trick: Key insight: don't move elements every operation. Only move when stack2 is empty. Each element crosses from stack1 to stack2 exactly once → amortized O(1).

title: Implement Stack using Queue
brute: Two queues. On push: enqueue to q1. On pop: move n-1 elements to q2, dequeue last from q1, swap q1 and q2.
better: Single queue: on push, enqueue then rotate (n-1) times so new element is at front.
optimal: One queue: push element, then dequeue and re-enqueue all previous elements. Front of queue = top of stack.
trick: After pushing new element: rotate queue by moving all other elements behind it. New element always at front = stack top. Pop = dequeue from front.

title: K-th largest element in stream
brute: Sort stream on each query. O(n log n) per query.
optimal: Min-heap of size k. Kth largest = min of top k largest = heap top. On add: push to heap. If size > k: pop minimum. Return heap.top().
trick: Min-heap of size k maintains k largest elements. Minimum of these k = kth largest overall. On each insertion: if heap grows > k, remove minimum (not in top k anymore).

title: Maximum Sum BST in Binary Tree
brute: For each node: check if subtree is valid BST, compute sum if valid.
optimal: Post-order DFS: for each subtree return (isBST, minVal, maxVal, sum). If both children are BST AND root.val > left.max AND root.val < right.min: current subtree is BST. Update global max sum.
trick: Return tuple (isBST, min, max, sum) from each node. BST condition: left.isBST AND right.isBST AND val > left.max AND val < right.min. Track global max sum separately.

title: Two Sum in BST
brute: Store all values in set. For each node: check if target-val exists in set.
optimal: HashSet approach: inorder traversal, for each value check if (target-value) in set, add value to set. OR: BST iterator (one forward, one backward) like two pointers.
pattern: BST + HashSet / Two pointer on BST
trick: Two pointer on BST: one iterator going inorder (ascending), one going reverse inorder (descending). Like two pointers on sorted array. Move pointers based on sum vs target.
similar: Find pair with given sum in BST

title: Construct BST from Preorder
brute: Insert each element into BST one by one. O(n²) worst case.
optimal: Recursive with valid range: f(preorder, idx, min, max). Current element = preorder[idx]. If out of range: return null. Recurse left with max=current. Recurse right with min=current.
trick: Use index as pointer (pass by reference/global). Range [min, max] defines valid BST node values. Left subtree: max=current.val. Right subtree: min=current.val.

title: BST Iterator
brute: Store entire inorder in array. O(n) space.
optimal: Controlled inorder traversal with stack. hasNext: stack not empty. next: pop node, add to result, push right child then go left from right child.
trick: Initialize by pushing all left nodes. next(): pop, process, then push all left nodes of right child. Each node pushed/popped exactly once → amortized O(1).

title: Largest BST in Binary Tree
brute: For each node: check if subtree is BST, compute size.
optimal: Post-order: return (isBST, size, min, max) for each subtree. If both children BST and root satisfies BST property: size = left.size + right.size + 1. Track global max size.
trick: Return tuple to avoid multiple traversals. Use INT_MIN and INT_MAX as initial min/max for leaf nodes. If not BST: return size=0 so parent also won't be BST.

title: Morris Traversal
brute: Recursive inorder. O(n) space for call stack.
better: Iterative with explicit stack. O(n) space.
optimal: Morris: if no left child: process current, go right. If left child: find inorder predecessor. If predecessor.right==null: thread it to current, go left. Else unthread, process current, go right.
trick: Temporarily modify tree structure to create threads (links) back to successor. This allows returning to parent without stack. Restore links after use. True O(1) space traversal.

title: Children Sum Property
brute: For each node: check if val == left.val + right.val.
optimal: DFS: check if node.val == left.val + right.val (for non-leaf nodes). Return false immediately if violated. Recurse on children.
trick: Base cases: null nodes return true. Leaf nodes return true (no children to sum). For internal nodes: check property and recurse. Short-circuit on failure.

title: All Nodes Distance K in Binary Tree
brute: BFS from target in full graph (treating tree as undirected).
optimal: Convert tree to undirected graph (adjacency list). BFS from target node for K levels. Collect all nodes at level K.
trick: Tree edges are one-directional. To find nodes K above target: need parent links. Build adjacency list first (treating as undirected graph). Then standard BFS for K hops.

title: Minimum time to burn a Binary Tree
brute: Simulate fire spread from target node.
optimal: Convert to undirected graph. BFS from target node. Time = number of BFS levels - 1 (time when last node burns).
trick: Same as 'All Nodes Distance K'. Fire spreads to all adjacent nodes simultaneously. BFS from source: each level = 1 second. Answer = max distance to any node.

title: Count Nodes in Complete Binary Tree
brute: DFS/BFS counting all nodes. O(n).
optimal: Use complete binary tree property: compute left height and right height. If equal: left subtree is perfect, count = 2^leftH - 1 + 1 + right subtree count. Else: right subtree perfect.
trick: Complete BT: left height == right height means left subtree is PERFECT (2^h - 1 nodes). Recursively count only the non-perfect subtree. O(log²n) beats O(n).
similar: Check if tree is Complete BT

title: Vertical Order Traversal
brute: BFS with coordinates, sort result.
optimal: BFS/DFS with (row, col) coordinates. TreeMap of col -> TreeMap of row -> sorted list of values. Root at (0,0). Left child: (row+1, col-1). Right: (row+1, col+1). Collect in order.
trick: Multiple nodes can be at same (row, col): sort by value. Use TreeMap for automatic column ordering. Inner TreeMap for row ordering within column.

title: Bottom View of Binary Tree
brute: N/A
optimal: BFS with horizontal distance (hd). Root hd=0, left child hd-1, right child hd+1. For each hd: store LAST seen node (BFS ensures bottom-to-top order reversed = last = bottom). TreeMap for ordering.
trick: Bottom view = LAST node seen at each horizontal distance (vs Top view = FIRST). BFS processes top-to-bottom so last updated at each hd = bottom view.

title: Boundary Traversal of Binary Tree
brute: N/A
optimal: Three parts: 1) Left boundary (top-down, excluding leaves). 2) All leaf nodes (left to right). 3) Right boundary (bottom-up, excluding leaves). Combine.
trick: Carefully avoid adding leaf nodes twice. Left boundary: go left preferring left child. Leaves: inorder traversal adding only leaves. Right boundary: go right, collect then reverse.

title: Maximum Width of Binary Tree
brute: BFS level by level, find max non-null span.
optimal: BFS with node indices (like heap indexing): root=0, left child=2*i, right child=2*i+1. Width at each level = last index - first index + 1. Normalize indices to prevent overflow.
trick: Null nodes between two valid nodes count as width. Heap indexing handles this. IMPORTANT: normalize each level's indices by subtracting first index to prevent integer overflow.

title: Kth Ancestor in Tree
brute: Find path from root to node. Return kth element from end.
optimal: Binary Lifting: parent[i][j] = 2^j th ancestor of node i. Precompute in O(n log n). For query: decompose k in binary, jump accordingly.
time_complexity: O(n log n) preprocess, O(log n) query
pattern: Binary Lifting — sparse table on tree
trick: 2^j ancestor = 2^(j-1) ancestor of 2^(j-1) ancestor. Table allows jumping powers of 2. For k: if bit j set in k: jump to 2^j ancestor, k -= 2^j. Repeat.

title: LCA using Binary Lifting
brute: Find paths from root to both nodes. First common node.
optimal: Binary Lifting: precompute ancestor table. For LCA: bring both nodes to same depth. Then jump both together until they meet.
time_complexity: O(n log n) preprocess, O(log n) query
trick: Step 1: make both nodes same depth (lift shallower one). Step 2: if same: that's LCA. Step 3: lift both together without making them equal — find highest non-common ancestor. Parent of that = LCA.

title: Euler Tour / Traversal
brute: N/A
optimal: DFS recording each node when first visited and when last left (entry and exit times). Creates a flat array representation of tree. Used with Segment Trees for range queries on trees.
trick: Entry time = first visit. Exit time = after all descendants processed. Subtree of node v = range [entry[v], exit[v]] in Euler tour. Enables O(log n) subtree queries with segment tree.

title: Segment Tree
brute: For range queries: iterate entire range. O(n) per query.
optimal: Build segment tree: each node stores aggregate (sum/min/max) of range. Build O(n). Point update O(log n). Range query O(log n).
pattern: Segment Tree — range queries with updates
trick: Use 4n size array (safe upper bound). Node i: left child = 2i, right child = 2i+1. Lazy propagation for range updates. Essential for competitive programming.

title: Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree)
brute: Prefix sum array. O(1) query but O(n) update.
optimal: BIT array indexed by LSB. Update: i += i & (-i) to propagate. Query prefix sum: i -= i & (-i) to collect. Range query = prefix(r) - prefix(l-1).
pattern: Fenwick Tree — efficient prefix sums with updates
trick: i & (-i) isolates lowest set bit. BIT stores partial sums, not prefix sums. Simpler to code than Segment Tree but less flexible (works for invertible operations like sum).
